flashcard.front: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter?
flashcard.back: The distal femoral physis on coronal images: OPEN physes = juvenile OCD (greater healing potential, more often managed conservatively); CLOSED physes = adult OCD (poorer healing, more often surgical). Caveat: in skeletally immature patients a high-T2 rim alone is unreliable for instability — it can be vascular granulation in a stable lesion.

fc-menisci-t0-1.stem: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface?

flashcard.front: What grade of intrameniscal signal reaches the articular surface?
flashcard.back: Grade 3. Grade 1 is a globular dot of mucinous degeneration, Grade 2 is linear intrameniscal signal not reaching the surface, and Grade 3 contacts the articular surface (= tear).

fc-menisci-t0-2.stem: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear?

flashcard.front: On PD / short-TE images, does intrameniscal signal have to be as bright as fluid to call a tear?
flashcard.back: No — on PD / short-TE, surface-reaching intermediate-to-high signal is enough; it need not approach fluid intensity. (Requiring fluid-bright signal is the T2 / long-TE criterion, which is why T2 is less sensitive.) The key is signal unequivocally reaching an articular surface on ≥2 consecutive images.

flashcard.front: What is the two-slice-touch rule?
flashcard.back: Meniscal signal must contact the articular surface on at least two consecutive sagittal images (at standard 3-4 mm slice thickness) to be diagnosed as a tear. This reduces false positives from volume averaging and truncation artifact.

fc-menisci-t1-2.stem: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T?

flashcard.front: Why does the two-slice-touch rule require caution at 3T?
flashcard.back: At 3T, magic angle effect and magnetization transfer can cause artifactually increased intrameniscal signal, particularly in the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus near the popliteal hiatus, mimicking tears.

fc-menisci-t2-1.stem: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small?

flashcard.front: Why are radial meniscal tears biomechanically significant even when small?
flashcard.back: Radial tears disrupt the circumferential hoop fibers of the meniscus, eliminating its ability to distribute axial load. This is true regardless of tear size.

fc-menisci-t2-2.stem: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest?

flashcard.front: What is the "ghost meniscus" sign and what tear type does it suggest?
flashcard.back: The meniscus appears absent or truncated on images through the tear. It is seen with radial tears (where the tear is parallel to the imaging plane) and with root tears on sagittal images.

fc-menisci-t2-3.stem: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate?

flashcard.front: What is the most common degenerative meniscal tear pattern and where does it typically originate?
flashcard.back: Horizontal (cleavage) tear, running parallel to the tibial plateau and splitting the meniscus into upper and lower leaves. It most commonly originates in the posterior horn of the medial meniscus.

fc-menisci-t3-1.stem: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure?

flashcard.front: A meniscal root tear is functionally equivalent to what procedure?
flashcard.back: Total meniscectomy. Root tears completely disrupt the circumferential hoop stresses that allow the meniscus to distribute axial load.

fc-menisci-t3-2.stem: What is the root tear triad?

flashcard.front: What is the root tear triad?
flashcard.back: Posterior medial root tear + meniscal extrusion (>3 mm) + subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle or tibial plateau. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause.

fc-menisci-t3-3.stem: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear?

flashcard.front: What is the "ghost sign" on sagittal images in the context of a root tear?
flashcard.back: Absence of the normal dark posterior horn root attachment adjacent to the PCL insertion on sagittal images. It indicates the root has been avulsed or disrupted.

fc-menisci-t4-1.stem: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees?

flashcard.front: What is a ramp lesion and how common is it in ACL-injured knees?
flashcard.back: A peripheral longitudinal tear of the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at the meniscocapsular junction. It is found in approximately 16-24% of ACL-deficient knees.

fc-menisci-t4-2.stem: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions?

flashcard.front: What is the reported MRI sensitivity for detecting ramp lesions?
flashcard.back: Only 48-65%. Ramp lesions are notoriously underdiagnosed on preoperative MRI, missed in up to half of cases. In every ACL tear, actively search the posterior meniscocapsular junction.

fc-menisci-t5-1.stem: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear?

flashcard.front: What are the three cardinal signs of a bucket-handle tear?
flashcard.back: Absent bow-tie sign (fewer than two consecutive body sagittal images), double PCL sign (displaced fragment parallel to the PCL), and flipped meniscus sign (fragment adjacent to the anterior horn making it appear disproportionately large).

fc-menisci-t5-2.stem: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body?

flashcard.front: How many consecutive sagittal images should show the normal bow-tie configuration of the meniscal body?
flashcard.back: At least two. Fewer than two consecutive bow-tie images suggests a bucket-handle tear with displacement of the central fragment.

fc-menisci-t6-1.stem: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic?

flashcard.front: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic?
flashcard.back: Measured on coronal images at the meniscal mid-body: distance from the outer tibial plateau margin to the outermost meniscal edge. Greater than 3 mm is pathologic; greater than 5 mm suggests severe extrusion with major loss of hoop stress function.

fc-menisci-t6-2.stem: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear?

flashcard.front: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear?
flashcard.back: Extrusion >3 mm is pathologic and should prompt an active search for a meniscal root tear on coronal and axial images; >5 mm indicates severe meniscal dysfunction with major loss of hoop-stress function.

fc-menisci-t7-1.stem: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears?

flashcard.front: What is the relationship between parameniscal cysts and meniscal tears?
flashcard.back: Most parameniscal cysts have an underlying meniscal tear acting as a one-way valve. The tear allows joint fluid to decompress into parameniscal tissues. The cyst may recur if only the cyst is treated without addressing the causative tear.

fc-menisci-t7-2.stem: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause?

flashcard.front: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause?
flashcard.back: Compression of the common peroneal nerve, presenting with foot drop.

fc-menisci-t8-1.stem: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing?

flashcard.front: After meniscal repair, what signal criterion distinguishes re-tear from expected postoperative healing?
flashcard.back: Fluid-bright signal (matching joint fluid on T2) reaching the articular surface indicates re-tear. Intermediate signal at the repair site represents expected healing granulation tissue and should not be overcalled.

fc-menisci-t8-2.stem: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI?

flashcard.front: After partial meniscectomy, what does the normal meniscal remnant look like on MRI?
flashcard.back: Truncated and blunted with a smooth, well-defined free edge and uniformly low signal. Persistent grade 3 signal extending to the new articular surface suggests re-tear.

fc-ligaments-t0-1.stem: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear?

flashcard.front: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear?
flashcard.back: Discontinuity of ligament fibers, abnormal morphology (wavy, lax, or horizontally oriented), and abnormal signal intensity (edema/hemorrhage on T2). A complete tear shows amorphous edematous tissue replacing the ligament.

fc-ligaments-t0-2.stem: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear.

flashcard.front: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear.
flashcard.back: Pivot-shift bone contusions (posterolateral tibial plateau + lateral femoral condyle sulcus), anterior tibial translation >5 mm, and PCL buckling. Others include deepened lateral femoral sulcus >1.5 mm, Segond fracture, and uncovered posterior horn sign.

fc-ligaments-t0-3.stem: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears?

flashcard.front: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears?
flashcard.back: A small avulsion of the lateral tibial rim representing anterolateral ligament avulsion. It is pathognomonic for ACL tear, with ACL injury present in approximately 75-100% of cases.

fc-ligaments-t1-1.stem: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI?

flashcard.front: How does ACL mucoid degeneration differ from an ACL tear on MRI?
flashcard.back: Mucoid degeneration shows a thickened, bulbous ACL with diffuse T2 signal but intact, continuous fibers ("celery stalk" appearance). Unlike tears, fiber tension and orientation are preserved, and secondary signs (bone contusions, tibial translation) are absent.

fc-ligaments-t2-1.stem: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI?

flashcard.front: What is the ACL graft ligamentization period, and how does it appear on MRI?
flashcard.back: During the first 6-12 months, the graft undergoes revascularization and remodeling with increased T2 signal. This is expected and should not be confused with graft failure. A mature graft (12-24 months) should be uniformly low signal.

fc-ligaments-t2-2.stem: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure?

flashcard.front: What is the most common technical error leading to ACL graft failure?
flashcard.back: Tunnel malposition. The two mechanisms differ: an anteriorly placed TIBIAL tunnel causes roof (notch) impingement in extension, while a too-anterior or vertical FEMORAL tunnel produces a non-isometric/vertical graft with persistent rotational (pivot-shift) instability. On sagittal MRI, the femoral tunnel should sit in the posterior aspect of the intercondylar notch.

fc-ligaments-t3-1.stem: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear?

flashcard.front: What is the classic mechanism for a PCL tear?
flashcard.back: A posterior-directed force on the proximal tibia with the knee flexed -- the "dashboard injury." Bone contusions are typically on the anterior tibial plateau.

fc-ligaments-t3-2.stem: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize?

flashcard.front: When does a PCL buckle anteriorly, and why is this important to recognize?
flashcard.back: The PCL may buckle anteriorly in the setting of an ACL tear with anterior tibial translation. This should not be confused with a primary PCL injury.

fc-ligaments-t4-1.stem: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings?

flashcard.front: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings?
flashcard.back: Grade I (sprain): periligamentous edema with intact fibers. Grade II (partial tear): partial discontinuity with surrounding edema. Grade III (complete tear): complete fiber disruption with fluid filling the gap.

fc-ligaments-t4-2.stem: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer?

flashcard.front: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer?
flashcard.back: The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) attaches directly to the medial meniscus. Deep MCL tears can cause meniscocapsular separation, which may require surgical repair -- a different management path than an isolated superficial MCL sprain.

fc-ligaments-t5-1.stem: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures?

flashcard.front: What are the three primary posterolateral corner (PLC) structures?
flashcard.back: LCL (fibular collateral ligament), popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament.

fc-ligaments-t5-2.stem: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate?

flashcard.front: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate?
flashcard.back: Avulsion of the fibular styloid. It is pathognomonic for a posterolateral corner injury.

fc-ligaments-t5-3.stem: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure?

flashcard.front: Why is an unrecognized PLC injury an important, frequently overlooked cause of cruciate graft failure?
flashcard.back: Unrecognized PLC injury allows persistent external rotation and varus laxity, which overloads cruciate ligament reconstructions. PLC injuries must be identified and addressed for successful graft outcomes.

fc-ligaments-t6-1.stem: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create?

flashcard.front: What is the primary structure of the posteromedial corner and what instability pattern does PMC injury create?
flashcard.back: The posterior oblique ligament (POL). PMC injury contributes to anteromedial rotatory instability (AMRI), and when combined with ACL tear, creates greater rotational instability than an isolated ACL tear.

fc-ligaments-t7-1.stem: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation?

flashcard.front: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation?
flashcard.back: Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle.

fc-ligaments-t7-2.stem: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn?

flashcard.front: At what location is the MPFL most commonly torn?
flashcard.back: At its attachments far more than mid-substance. Classic adult teaching is femoral-sided predominance (~50%, Balcarek 2010), but in skeletally immature patients the patellar attachment predominates (~80-95%, Askenberger 2016) — scrutinize BOTH ends.

fc-ligaments-t7-3.stem: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed?

flashcard.front: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed?
flashcard.back: Patella alta (Insall-Salvati ratio >1.2), trochlear dysplasia (Dejour classification), and increased TT-TG distance (>20 mm is pathologic).

fc-ligaments-t8-1.stem: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise?

flashcard.front: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise?
flashcard.back: Combined ACL + PCL disruption, even if the knee has spontaneously reduced. This carries a limb-threatening risk of occult popliteal artery injury, requiring urgent vascular assessment.

fc-ligaments-t8-2.stem: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed?

flashcard.front: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed?
flashcard.back: Popliteal artery injury must be excluded urgently. MRI signs include loss of the normal flow void, perivascular hematoma, and intimal irregularity; CTA/MRA is used when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal, or per trauma protocol.

fc-extensor-synovium-t0-1.stem: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears?

flashcard.front: Which layer of the quadriceps tendon is most commonly involved in partial tears?
flashcard.back: The deep layer (vastus intermedius). On sagittal images, look for a fluid-signal defect at the deep aspect of the tendon near the patellar insertion. The superficial fibers may appear intact.

fc-extensor-synovium-t0-2.stem: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear?

flashcard.front: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear?
flashcard.back: A complete tear shows full-thickness discontinuity with proximal retraction of the tendon stump, patella baja (low-riding patella), and often a large hemorrhagic joint effusion.

fc-extensor-synovium-t1-1.stem: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI?

flashcard.front: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI?
flashcard.back: Tendinopathy shows thickening and intermediate signal (not fluid-bright on T2) with intact continuity. A partial tear shows a discrete fluid-signal defect within the tendon. This distinction matters: tendinopathy is managed conservatively; significant partial tears may need surgery.

fc-extensor-synovium-t1-2.stem: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon?

flashcard.front: After bone-patellar tendon-bone graft harvest for ACL reconstruction, what is the expected appearance of the patellar tendon?
flashcard.back: A thinned central portion of the tendon with surrounding scar tissue. This is a normal postoperative finding and should not be called a tear.

fc-extensor-synovium-t2-1.stem: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture?

flashcard.front: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture?
flashcard.back: Bipartite patella has smooth, well-corticated margins, is located at the superolateral pole (~75% of cases), and is bilateral in ~50%. Fractures have sharp, irregular margins, most commonly occur at the mid-patella, and are usually unilateral.

fc-extensor-synovium-t2-2.stem: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI?

flashcard.front: What is a painful bipartite patella and what finding indicates it on MRI?
flashcard.back: When a bipartite patella becomes symptomatic from stress or trauma, bone marrow edema is seen at the synchondrosis between the accessory ossicle and the patella.

fc-extensor-synovium-t3-1.stem: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI?

flashcard.front: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI?
flashcard.back: Lipohemarthrosis is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture. It appears as a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa: the superior fat layer is bright on T1, and the inferior layer is serous or hemorrhagic fluid.

fc-extensor-synovium-t3-2.stem: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do?

flashcard.front: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do?
flashcard.back: Perform a dedicated second-look fracture search of the tibial plateaus, patella, and femoral condyles. Subtle lateral tibial plateau depression fractures are the most commonly missed finding.

fc-extensor-synovium-t4-1.stem: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for?

flashcard.front: Where does a Baker's cyst arise and what associated pathology should you search for?
flashcard.back: Baker's cysts arise between the medial head of the gastrocnemius and the semimembranosus tendon posteromedially. They are commonly secondary to intra-articular pathology (meniscal tears, cartilage degeneration, inflammatory arthropathy).

fc-extensor-synovium-t4-2.stem: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic?

flashcard.front: What clinical condition does a ruptured Baker's cyst mimic?
flashcard.back: Deep venous thrombosis (pseudothrombophlebitis). On MRI, a ruptured Baker's cyst shows inferior extension of fluid along the medial gastrocnemius fascial planes.

fc-extensor-synovium-t5-1.stem: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic?

flashcard.front: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic?
flashcard.back: Located approximately 4-5 cm below the medial joint line, deep to the sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus tendons. It commonly mimics medial meniscal tear or MCL injury clinically.

fc-extensor-synovium-t6-1.stem: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)?

flashcard.front: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)?
flashcard.back: Low signal intensity on all pulse sequences due to hemosiderin deposition, with "blooming" artifact on gradient echo sequences. Diffuse PVNS also shows bone erosions on both sides of the joint with relative preservation of joint space.

fc-extensor-synovium-t6-2.stem: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI?

flashcard.front: How do you distinguish PVNS from synovial chondromatosis on MRI?
flashcard.back: PVNS shows low signal on all sequences with GRE blooming (hemosiderin). Synovial chondromatosis shows cartilage-signal bodies (intermediate T1, high T2) without blooming, often with ring-and-arc calcification.

fc-extensor-synovium-t7-1.stem: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain?

flashcard.front: On fluid-sensitive MRI, how do you distinguish a Grade 1 from a Grade 3 muscle strain?
flashcard.back: Grade 1: feathery/stellate perifascial edema with intact architecture and NO fiber gap. Grade 3: complete fiber discontinuity with a retracted wavy/serpentine stump and hematoma filling the gap.

fc-extensor-synovium-t7-2.stem: What are the two tendinous components of the proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity, and what must the report specify in an avulsion?

flashcard.front: What are the two tendinous components of the proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity, and what must the report specify in an avulsion?
flashcard.back: (1) The conjoint tendon (biceps femoris long head + semitendinosus fused into one tendon) and (2) the separate semimembranosus tendon. Report which/how many components are torn, the retraction distance in cm, and proximity to the sciatic nerve.

fc-extensor-synovium-t7-3.stem: What is the signature MRI finding of medial gastrocnemius 'tennis leg,' and which two diagnoses must always be on the differential?

flashcard.front: What is the signature MRI finding of medial gastrocnemius 'tennis leg,' and which two diagnoses must always be on the differential?
flashcard.back: Fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Always exclude DVT (check the popliteal vein) and ruptured Baker's cyst (plantaris rupture is a less common mimic).

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t0-1.stem: Why is a missed posterior medial meniscal root tear so consequential?

flashcard.front: Why is a missed posterior medial meniscal root tear so consequential?
flashcard.back: It is functionally equivalent to total meniscectomy, disrupting circumferential hoop stresses. If missed, it leads to subchondral insufficiency fractures, accelerated cartilage loss, progressive varus malalignment, and eventual arthroplasty.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t0-2.stem: When you see a subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle, what must you evaluate?

flashcard.front: When you see a subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle, what must you evaluate?
flashcard.back: The posterior medial meniscal root. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause. If you only report the fracture without identifying the root tear, the underlying problem will not be addressed.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t1-1.stem: What should you include in every ACL tear report regarding ramp lesions?

flashcard.front: What should you include in every ACL tear report regarding ramp lesions?
flashcard.back: A specific statement about the presence or absence of a ramp lesion. Since MRI sensitivity is only ~50-65%, recommend intraoperative assessment through a posteromedial portal when findings are equivocal.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t2-1.stem: If you see fewer than two bow-tie images on sagittal sequences, what should you do next?

flashcard.front: If you see fewer than two bow-tie images on sagittal sequences, what should you do next?
flashcard.back: Immediately suspect a bucket-handle tear and search for the displaced fragment: in the intercondylar notch (double PCL sign) or flipped against the anterior horn (flipped meniscus sign). Check coronal images for a truncated donor site.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t3-1.stem: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability, and why is T1 signal unreliable for this assessment?

flashcard.front: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability, and why is T1 signal unreliable for this assessment?
flashcard.back: Fluid-signal intensity (matching joint fluid) on T2 at the fragment-bone interface indicates undermining and instability. A low-signal rim on T1 is nonspecific -- it can represent either healing fibrous tissue or an unstable cleavage plane.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t4-1.stem: Why is failure to diagnose a PLC injury the leading cause of cruciate graft failure?

flashcard.front: Why is failure to diagnose a PLC injury the leading cause of cruciate graft failure?
flashcard.back: Unrecognized PLC injury allows persistent external rotation and varus laxity, which overloads the cruciate graft. All three PLC structures (LCL, popliteus tendon, popliteofibular ligament) must be systematically evaluated in every cruciate injury.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t4-2.stem: What fracture is pathognomonic for posterolateral corner injury?

flashcard.front: What fracture is pathognomonic for posterolateral corner injury?
flashcard.back: Arcuate fracture -- avulsion of the fibular styloid. This finding should never be dismissed as incidental.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t5-1.stem: After diagnosing lateral patellar dislocation with MPFL tear, what is the complete evaluation checklist?

flashcard.front: After diagnosing lateral patellar dislocation with MPFL tear, what is the complete evaluation checklist?
flashcard.back: MPFL tear location, osteochondral injury and loose bodies (check suprapatellar pouch, lateral gutter, posterior recesses), trochlear dysplasia, patella alta (Insall-Salvati >1.2), and TT-TG distance (>20 mm pathologic).

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t6-1.stem: What finding is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture?

flashcard.front: What finding is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture?
flashcard.back: Lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa). When identified, perform a systematic search for fractures of the tibial plateau, patella, and femoral condyles.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t7-1.stem: What is a Segond fracture, and how does a reverse Segond fracture differ in its ligamentous association?

flashcard.front: What is a Segond fracture, and how does a reverse Segond fracture differ in its ligamentous association?
flashcard.back: Segond fracture: lateral tibial rim avulsion, pathognomonic for ACL tear. Reverse Segond fracture: medial tibial rim avulsion, associated with PCL tears rather than ACL tears.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t8-1.stem: Why does combined ACL + PCL disruption require emergent vascular assessment?

flashcard.front: Why does combined ACL + PCL disruption require emergent vascular assessment?
flashcard.back: Combined cruciate disruption equals a knee dislocation pattern until proven otherwise. Popliteal artery injury can be occult after spontaneous reduction, so urgent vascular assessment is required; CTA/MRA is used when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal, or per trauma protocol.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t9-1.stem: How long after meniscal repair can increased signal at the repair site persist as a normal finding?

flashcard.front: How long after meniscal repair can increased signal at the repair site persist as a normal finding?
flashcard.back: At least 12 months, and it may persist indefinitely. Only fluid-bright signal (matching joint fluid on T2) reaching the articular surface constitutes a definitive re-tear.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t9-2.stem: At what time point should a mature ACL graft appear uniformly low signal, and what signal indicates failure?

flashcard.front: At what time point should a mature ACL graft appear uniformly low signal, and what signal indicates failure?
flashcard.back: By 12-18 months. Fluid-signal intensity within the graft substance, discontinuity, or recurrence of secondary signs (anterior tibial translation, new pivot-shift contusions) indicates graft failure.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t10-1.stem: What three key questions should your knee MRI report answer for the surgeon?

flashcard.front: What three key questions should your knee MRI report answer for the surgeon?
flashcard.back: (1) What is the diagnosis? (2) What associated injuries are present? (3) Does anything change my planned approach? Anticipate the surgeon's decision points and address them proactively.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t10-2.stem: For meniscal tears, what factors should you address to help the surgeon determine repairability?

flashcard.front: For meniscal tears, what factors should you address to help the surgeon determine repairability?
flashcard.back: Location (peripheral red zone = repairable vs. central white zone = resection), morphology (vertical longitudinal = repairable vs. complex = usually resection), displacement status, and acuity.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t11-1.stem: Name four findings on knee MRI that require urgent or direct communication with the referring clinician.

flashcard.front: Name four findings on knee MRI that require urgent or direct communication with the referring clinician.
flashcard.back: Knee dislocation/multiligament injury (needs urgent vascular assessment), locked bucket-handle tear, displaced osteochondral fragments in a weight-bearing compartment, and suspected septic arthritis.

fc-top-10-dont-miss-t11-2.stem: What must you document per ACR practice parameters when directly communicating a significant finding?

flashcard.front: What must you document per ACR practice parameters when directly communicating a significant finding?
flashcard.back: Who was contacted, the method of communication, and the time. This documentation should be included in your report.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-mri-basics-1-pre.stem: A 24-year-old recreational runner has medial knee pain after ramping up mileage. On the coronal T1-weighted image a thin curvilinear low-signal line parallels the medial femoral condyle subchondral plate, and you suspect an early subchondral stress (fatigue) fracture. Which additional sequence best confirms the surrounding reactive marrow edema?

options.text: Non-fat-saturated T2, where the edema blends in against the adjacent bright marrow fat
Fat-saturated PD/T2, where edema is fluid-bright against suppressed fat
Gradient-echo, which is the most sensitive sequence for marrow edema
Non-fat-saturated T1, where the dark fracture line itself proves the edema

knee-mri::prepost-kn-mri-basics-1-pre.explanation: Marrow edema is water-rich, so it is conspicuously fluid-bright on fluid-sensitive fat-saturated sequences (PD/T2 FS) where the normally bright fat signal is suppressed, maximizing contrast. Option D confuses the dark fracture line on T1 (which depicts the fracture, not the surrounding edema) with proof of edema, and option C reflects the common misconception that gradient-echo is sensitive for marrow edema when in fact susceptibility blooming makes it insensitive and unreliable here.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-mri-basics-1-post.stem: A 30-year-old presents after a twisting injury. A focal area of the lateral femoral condyle marrow is dark on T1, and you must decide whether it represents acute trauma-related edema or simply fatty (yellow) marrow. Which cross-sequence signal behavior confirms it is edema rather than fat?

options.text: It follows fat: bright on T1 and dark on fat-saturated PD/T2
It stays dark on fat-saturated PD/T2, matching cortical bone
It enhances only after IV gadolinium and is otherwise occult on all unenhanced sequences
It is dark on T1 yet becomes fluid-bright (high signal) on fat-saturated PD/T2

knee-mri::prepost-kn-mri-basics-1-post.explanation: True marrow edema contains increased water, so it is low on T1 and turns fluid-bright on fat-saturated PD/T2 while normal marrow fat is suppressed; this cross-sequence behavior is what distinguishes edema from fat. Option A describes fatty marrow, which follows fat on every sequence and is not edema, and option C reflects an over-reliance-on-contrast misconception, since marrow edema is readily seen on unenhanced fluid-sensitive sequences and does not require gadolinium.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-normal-variants-1-p.stem: On a sagittal PD image you see an oblique low-signal band coursing just anterior and inferior to the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus, creating an apparent cleft. Before calling an anterior horn tear, what is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Transverse (intermeniscal) ligament producing a pseudotear at its junction with the meniscus
A radial tear of the lateral meniscus anterior horn
Discoid meniscus with central degeneration
Popliteus tendon within the popliteal hiatus

knee-mri::prepost-kn-normal-variants-1-p.explanation: The transverse intermeniscal ligament connects the anterior horns of the two menisci, and the fat-filled cleft at its attachment to the lateral anterior horn classically mimics a tear; tracing the band to the contralateral meniscus on adjacent slices confirms the variant. Option D (popliteus in the hiatus) mimics a POSTERIOR horn tear, not the anterior horn, and option B fails because a true radial tear would not be traceable as a continuous band crossing to the medial meniscus.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-normal-variants-1-p.stem: On sagittal images a thin low-signal structure runs obliquely from the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus toward the medial femoral condyle, with a cleft at its meniscal origin that looks like a posterior horn tear. How do you confirm this is a normal variant?

options.text: Trace it to its femoral attachment near the PCL, identifying a meniscofemoral ligament (Humphrey or Wrisberg)
Confirm grade-3 signal reaching the surface on two consecutive slices
Note that the cleft disappears only on the coronal plane
Identify the popliteus tendon passing through the popliteal hiatus

knee-mri::prepost-kn-normal-variants-1-p.explanation: The meniscofemoral ligaments of Humphrey (anterior to PCL) and Wrisberg (posterior to PCL) arise from the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus and insert on the medial femoral condyle; the fat cleft at their meniscal origin mimics a posterior horn tear, and tracing the band to its femoral attachment confirms the variant. Option B describes criteria for a true tear (the wrong conclusion here), and option D names a different lateral-meniscus pseudotear (popliteal hiatus) rather than the structure described.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-search-reporting-1-.stem: You confirm a complete ACL tear on a young pivoting athlete's MRI. Applying a systematic search, which structure must you deliberately re-inspect next because missing it would change surgical planning?

options.text: The posteromedial meniscocapsular junction for a ramp lesion and the posterior meniscal roots
The quadriceps tendon insertion for tendinosis
The prepatellar soft tissues for bursitis
The proximal tibiofibular joint for a ganglion

knee-mri::prepost-kn-search-reporting-1-.explanation: ACL tears are highly associated with ramp lesions at the posteromedial meniscocapsular junction and with posterior root tears, both of which are easily overlooked yet directly alter the surgeon's repair plan, so they belong in the directed post-ACL search and impression. Options C and D are real findings but are not management-changing companions of an ACL tear, and quadriceps tendinosis (option B) reflects satisfaction-of-search drift to an unrelated, low-yield structure.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-search-reporting-1-.stem: Your primary finding is a displaced bucket-handle tear of the medial meniscus with a fragment in the intercondylar notch. Following a disciplined search, what is the most important next structure to evaluate before finalizing the impression?

options.text: The Hoffa fat pad for a focal ganglion cyst
The iliotibial band for distal friction syndrome
The ACL, because bucket-handle displacement is frequently associated with ACL insufficiency
The pes anserine bursa for fluid

knee-mri::prepost-kn-search-reporting-1-.explanation: A displaced fragment in the notch can mimic or obscure the ACL, and bucket-handle tears are commonly associated with ACL injury, so the disciplined reader must verify ACL integrity (and avoid the 'double-PCL'/pseudo-intact ACL pitfall) before the impression. Options B and D are incidental, non-management-changing checks that represent drifting away from the high-yield, directed search prompted by the primary finding.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-1-.stem: A 19-year-old soccer player felt a pop while cutting. MRI shows kissing contusions at the lateral femoral condyle (sulcus terminalis region) and the posterolateral tibial plateau. Which ligament is most likely torn and should be scrutinized?

options.text: Posterior cruciate ligament
Anterior cruciate ligament
Medial patellofemoral ligament
Deep medial collateral ligament

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-1-.explanation: The pivot-shift mechanism drives the lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau together, producing this kissing-contusion pattern that is the hallmark of ACL rupture. The PCL (option A) is associated with an anterior proximal tibia contusion from a dashboard blow, and the MPFL (option C) pairs with a medial patella plus anterolateral femoral condyle pattern of transient patellar dislocation, both distinct mechanisms.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-1-.stem: A teenager dislocated the patella while pivoting, then it spontaneously reduced. Which combination of marrow edema and the associated structure to evaluate fits this mechanism?

options.text: Anterior proximal tibia edema; evaluate the PCL
Lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral plateau edema; evaluate the ACL
Medial patellar facet and anterolateral femoral condyle edema; evaluate the medial patellofemoral ligament
Medial femoral condyle subchondral edema; evaluate the deep MCL

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-1-.explanation: Transient lateral patellar dislocation produces a characteristic bone-bruise pair on the medial patellar facet (from MPFL avulsion impaction) and the anterolateral femoral condyle (from contact during relocation), so the MPFL must be assessed along with the search for osteochondral fragments. Option B is the pivot-shift/ACL pattern and option A is the dashboard/PCL pattern, both representing different injury mechanisms that trainees often conflate with patellar dislocation.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-2-.stem: A 68-year-old woman has acute medial knee pain with no significant trauma. MRI shows a focal subchondral low-signal line paralleling the medial femoral condyle articular surface with surrounding marrow edema. Which feature best distinguishes a subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK) from a simple traumatic contusion?

options.text: Diffuse, ill-defined marrow edema with no discrete subchondral line and an antecedent impact
A discrete subchondral low-signal fracture line subjacent to the articular surface with adjacent edema, often without notable trauma
Edema confined to the patella after a dashboard injury
Bright T1 fatty signal replacing the subchondral bone

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-2-.explanation: SIFK is defined by a discrete subchondral fracture line (low signal on all sequences) hugging the articular surface with surrounding edema, typically in older or osteoporotic patients without significant trauma. A traumatic contusion (option A) shows geographic, ill-defined edema WITHOUT a subchondral line and follows a known impact; option D describes benign fatty marrow, not an acute insufficiency fracture, a distinction trainees frequently miss.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-2-.stem: A 70-year-old man develops sudden medial knee pain. MRI shows extensive medial femoral condyle marrow edema, a subchondral focus, and overlying articular surface flattening. Which finding most supports subchondral insufficiency fracture (formerly 'spontaneous osteonecrosis,' SONK) rather than true avascular osteonecrosis?

options.text: A linear subchondral fracture line with adjacent edema and articular surface collapse, in an osteoporotic patient without steroid or alcohol history
A serpentine double-line sign demarcating a wedge-shaped epiphyseal infarct
Diffuse patchy marrow signal sparing the subchondral region
Bilateral symmetric metaphyseal infarcts

knee-mri::prepost-kn-marrow-mechanism-2-.explanation: Current teaching reframes 'SONK' as a subchondral insufficiency fracture: a linear subchondral fracture with adjacent edema and possible surface collapse in an older/osteoporotic patient, without the classic AVN risk factors. The serpentine double-line sign and discrete wedge-shaped or metaphyseal infarcts (options B and D) characterize true avascular necrosis, which trainees often incorrectly equate with this insufficiency-fracture presentation.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-1-pre.stem: A 58-year-old presents after a deep squat with acute medial knee pain. Coronal PD fat-sat MRI shows a radial-oriented avulsion at the posterior root insertion of the medial meniscus, and on the coronal image the meniscal body is displaced 4 mm beyond the tibial margin. Functionally, how should this finding be characterized in your report?

options.text: A stable degenerative intrasubstance change that requires no specific comment
A peripheral longitudinal tear that is likely to heal given the vascular zone
Loss of meniscal hoop-stress function, biomechanically equivalent to a near-total meniscectomy
An isolated meniscocapsular separation without effect on load distribution

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-1-pre.explanation: A posterior root avulsion uncouples the circumferential collagen ring, so the meniscus can no longer convert axial load into hoop (circumferential) stress; the joint then behaves as if the meniscus were absent, producing rapid compartment overload — hence the report must flag it as functionally a meniscectomy. Option B reflects the common error of treating a root avulsion like a healable peripheral longitudinal tear; the root is an avulsion of the anchor, not a vertical tear in vascular tissue. Option A misclassifies a mechanically catastrophic lesion as benign degeneration, and D confuses a meniscocapsular (ramp-type) injury with the root, which has very different load consequences.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-1-post.stem: A 61-year-old reports a pop while gardening. Sagittal images show fluid signal cleaving the posterior horn of the medial meniscus at its tibial insertion, and on the coronal image the body extrudes 5 mm medial to the tibial plateau edge. How does this lesion alter the biomechanics of the involved compartment?

options.text: It mildly stiffens the meniscus but preserves overall load transmission
It abolishes hoop-stress generation, transferring load directly to articular cartilage as in a meniscectomized knee
It only affects rotational stability, leaving axial load distribution intact
It produces a self-limited synovitis without measurable load redistribution

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-1-post.explanation: Detachment of the posterior root releases the circumferential fibers, so axial compressive load is no longer converted into hoop stress and is instead dumped onto the chondral surface — the same biomechanical state as a total meniscectomy, which is why extrusion (here 5 mm) is reported. Option A understates the lesion by treating a complete root avulsion as a partial stiffening change. Options C and D reflect misconceptions that the root governs only rotation or is merely an inflammatory finding, when in fact it is the principal anchor for compartment load-bearing.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-2-pre.stem: A 47-year-old has lateral joint-line pain. On sequential sagittal PD fat-sat images the lateral meniscus shows a segment where the normal triangular meniscal tissue abruptly vanishes, leaving a 'ghost' of absent meniscus, and the coronal image shows a vertical cleft splitting the meniscus perpendicular to its free edge. Which tear morphology does this pattern indicate?

options.text: A radial tear, with the 'cleft/ghost meniscus' sign reflecting a tear perpendicular to the circumferential fibers
A peripheral longitudinal tear paralleling the meniscal circumference
An intrasubstance (grade-2) degeneration not reaching the surface
A meniscocapsular (ramp) separation at the posteromedial corner

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-2-pre.explanation: A radial tear runs perpendicular to the long axis of the meniscus, cutting across the circumferential collagen bundles; on the plane where the tear is in-plane the meniscus appears truncated or absent (the 'ghost meniscus' sign), with a vertical cleft on the orthogonal plane. Option B describes a longitudinal tear, which parallels rather than crosses the circumferential fibers and does not produce a ghost segment, and option C is intrasubstance signal that does not reach a surface, so it cannot produce a through-and-through cleft. Option D is a peripheral capsular separation on the medial side, a different location and morphology entirely.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-2-post.stem: On a knee MRI, the medial meniscus body shows fluid-bright signal oriented perpendicular to its free margin on the coronal image, and on the adjacent sagittal slice the meniscus body looks shortened with a missing segment. The remaining meniscus is otherwise normal in position. How is this lesion best classified, and what is its key biomechanical consequence?

options.text: A peripheral longitudinal tear; it usually preserves hoop-stress function
A parameniscal cyst; it indicates a horizontal-cleavage tear with fluid tracking
A bucket-handle tear; the fragment has displaced into the notch
A radial tear extending to the free edge; it disrupts circumferential fibers and compromises hoop-stress distribution

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-2-post.explanation: A cleft perpendicular to the free edge with a truncated/absent meniscal segment on the orthogonal plane is the signature of a radial tear; because it transects the circumferential collagen bundles, a full-thickness radial tear disrupts hoop-stress generation much like a root tear and predisposes to extrusion and chondral overload. Option A mislabels the perpendicular cleft as a longitudinal tear, which parallels the fibers and does preserve hoop stress; option C invents a displaced bucket-handle fragment in the notch that is not described here. Option B mistakes a through-substance radial cleft for a parameniscal cyst, which would be a rounded T2-bright collection adjacent to a horizontal tear.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-3-pre.stem: A 19-year-old soccer player has a locked knee. Sagittal MRI shows a second low-signal band lying anterior and parallel to the posterior cruciate ligament within the intercondylar notch. The medial meniscus appears truncated. Which diagnosis does this fragment configuration indicate?

options.text: A bucket-handle meniscal tear with the fragment displaced into the intercondylar notch
A mucoid-degenerated PCL producing a thickened ligament
A meniscal root tear with posterior extrusion of the meniscal body
A torn meniscofemoral ligament of Wrisberg mimicking a second ligament

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-3-pre.explanation: A fragment lying parallel and anterior to the PCL — the 'double-PCL sign' — is the hallmark of a displaced bucket-handle tear, with the inner meniscal fragment flipped into the notch and a correspondingly truncated parent meniscus. Option B reflects the misconception that a doubled appearance equals PCL pathology, but mucoid degeneration thickens the PCL itself rather than adding a separate band. Option C describes extrusion (peripheral, not into the notch), and D confuses a normal meniscofemoral ligament with a displaced fragment; the Wrisberg ligament runs posterior to the PCL and is much thinner than a bucket-handle fragment.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-3-post.stem: Reviewing a knee MRI, you see grade-3 linear signal reaching the inferior articular surface of the medial meniscus posterior horn on two consecutive sagittal images, and the same defect is again visible reaching the surface on the coronal sequence. How should this be reported under the two-slice-touch criterion?

options.text: Indeterminate, because surface signal must be confirmed on three or more contiguous slices
A normal magic-angle artifact, since linear signal can be artifactual at the posterior horn
A definite meniscal tear, since surface-reaching signal is present on two images and corroborated on a second plane
An intrasubstance grade-2 signal that does not warrant a tear diagnosis

knee-mri::prepost-kn-menisci-3-post.explanation: The two-slice-touch rule diagnoses a tear when grade-3 signal contacts a meniscal surface on at least two images, and cross-plane confirmation (here on the coronal) further increases confidence — so this is a definite tear. Option A overstates the threshold by demanding three slices, and option D mislabels surface-reaching grade-3 signal as intrasubstance grade-2, which by definition does not reach the surface. Option B invokes magic-angle artifact, which occurs at ~55 degrees to B0 on short-TE sequences and does not reach an articular surface on fluid-sensitive images across two planes.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.stem: A 34-year-old has a well-corticated osteochondral lesion of the lateral femoral condyle. On the fat-sat T2 image, a continuous line of fluid-equivalent high signal undercuts the entire fragment at its interface with the parent bone. Which conclusion about stability is best supported?

options.text: The lesion is stable because the fragment retains its corticated margin
The lesion is unstable, as a fluid-signal rim completely separating the fragment indicates loss of osseous bridging
Stability cannot be assessed without intravenous contrast in every case
The fluid line represents normal red-marrow signal and is reassuring

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.explanation: A T2 fluid-equivalent rim that completely encircles the fragment interface signifies fluid tracking between fragment and donor site (loss of osseous continuity) and is a primary MRI sign of an unstable osteochondral lesion, alongside subchondral cysts, an overlying cartilage breach, and a displaced fragment. Option A is the classic trap that a corticated margin equals stability, whereas a complete high-signal rim outweighs that reassurance. Option D confuses bright fluid at the interface with normal hematopoietic marrow (which is intermediate, not fluid-bright), and C overstates the role of contrast, which is adjunctive rather than mandatory.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.stem: A 13-year-old gymnast with open physes has a medial femoral condyle osteochondral lesion showing a thin band of high T2 signal at the fragment interface. There is no subchondral cyst, the overlying cartilage is intact, and the fragment is in situ. How should you interpret the interface signal in this patient?

options.text: The interface signal indicates an unstable lesion warranting surgical fixation
The lesion is stable because skeletally immature patients heal reliably
Interface hyperintensity is less specific for instability in juveniles, so combine it with other signs and clinical findings before calling it unstable
Open physes make MRI uninterpretable for osteochondral lesions

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.explanation: In juvenile osteochondritis dissecans with open physes, T2 interface hyperintensity can reflect vascular granulation tissue in a healing, still-stable lesion, so it is less specific than in adults; the call requires corroborating signs (a surrounding cyst, encircling fluid rim, cartilage breach, or displacement) plus clinical correlation. Option A applies the adult fluid-rim rule too rigidly to a child, the most common pediatric pitfall, while option B errs the opposite way by assuming immaturity guarantees stability. Option D is incorrect because MRI remains the primary tool — it simply must be interpreted with the juvenile caveat in mind.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.stem: On a fat-suppressed PD image of the trochlea, you see a focal fluid-bright defect in the articular cartilage that extends through the full cartilage thickness down to, but not into, the subchondral bone plate. How should this chondral lesion be graded and reported?

options.text: A superficial partial-thickness defect involving less than 50% of cartilage depth
Subchondral marrow edema only, with the cartilage intact
An osteochondral fracture, because the subchondral bone is involved
A full-thickness chondral defect reaching the subchondral plate without bony involvement

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.explanation: Fluid signal filling a defect that spans the entire cartilage depth to the subchondral bone, but with the bone plate intact, is a full-thickness (modified Outerbridge/ICRS grade 4) chondral lesion — it is reported as full-thickness because cartilage loss reaches the plate, yet it remains a chondral, not osteochondral, injury. Option A undercalls a full-thickness defect as a partial one (<50% depth). Option C overcalls it as an osteochondral fracture; the subchondral bone is preserved here, distinguishing a pure chondral defect from an osteochondral one. Option B ignores the visible cartilage defect entirely.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.stem: A 27-year-old runner has knee pain. Fat-sat PD images show a focal cartilage irregularity at the medial femoral condyle where joint fluid undercuts a flap of cartilage, but the deepest fibers of cartilage and the underlying bone remain in contact with the plate. Which grade best describes this lesion?

options.text: A partial-thickness chondral defect (cartilage flap/fissure) not extending to the subchondral bone
A full-thickness defect with exposed subchondral bone
An unstable osteochondral lesion with a displaced osseous fragment
Normal cartilage with magic-angle artifact simulating a defect

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cartilage-osteochon.explanation: Fluid undercutting a cartilage flap while the deep cartilage fibers still abut the subchondral plate is a partial-thickness defect (a fissure or delamination flap), graded below full-thickness because the lesion does not reach bare bone. Option B overcalls the depth — there is no exposed subchondral bone — and option C invents an osseous fragment that is not present, confusing a chondral flap with an osteochondral lesion. Option D dismisses a genuine fluid-filled defect as magic-angle artifact, which would not show joint fluid tracking under a flap on fluid-sensitive sequences.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-1-pre.stem: A 22-year-old soccer player has an acute non-contact pivoting injury. On the sagittal proton-density fat-saturated images, the radiologist wants to confirm a complete ACL tear using the most specific (primary) imaging criterion. Which finding most directly establishes the diagnosis?

options.text: A curvilinear cortical avulsion at the lateral proximal tibia (Segond fragment)
Anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur of more than 7 mm
Bone-marrow edema in the lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau
Full-thickness discontinuity of the ACL fibers with intervening high T2 signal in the expected ligament course

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-1-pre.explanation: The direct/primary sign of a complete ACL tear is frank fiber discontinuity with abnormal T2 signal filling the expected ligament course; this is the most specific criterion. Anterior tibial translation (B) and pivot-shift kissing contusions in the lateral femoral condyle/posterolateral tibia (C) are secondary signs that support but do not by themselves prove a tear. A Segond fragment (A) is a lateral capsular avulsion strongly associated with ACL injury but is itself only an indirect marker, not the primary ligamentous sign.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-1-post.stem: A 30-year-old basketball player landed awkwardly and felt a pop. On MRI the radiologist must decide which single finding constitutes the direct (primary) evidence of a complete ACL tear rather than a supportive secondary sign. Which observation provides that direct evidence?

options.text: A deep lateral femoral notch (terminal sulcus) deeper than 1.5 mm
Buckling of the PCL into an acute angulated (wavy) configuration
Uncovering of the posterior horn of the lateral meniscus by the tibia
Complete absence of intact ACL fibers spanning the notch with edematous signal replacing the ligament

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-1-post.explanation: Direct visualization of absent/discontinuous ACL fibers replaced by edematous signal across the notch is the primary sign that defines a complete tear. A deep lateral femoral notch (A), a buckled PCL (B), and posterior uncovering of the lateral meniscus (C) are all secondary signs reflecting anterior tibial translation; trainees often over-rely on these, but they only suggest ACL incompetence and can occur with chronic laxity, so they cannot independently establish an acute complete tear.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-2-pre.stem: A 48-year-old runner has chronic vague posterior knee pain with no instability on exam. MRI shows a thickened ACL with intermediate T2 signal interspersed between intact, continuous, well-tensioned fibers, giving a striated appearance, and there is no anterior tibial translation. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Acute complete ACL tear
Chronic partial-thickness ACL tear with fiber retraction
Mucoid degeneration of the ACL
ACL ganglion cyst causing fiber rupture

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-2-pre.explanation: Mucoid degeneration produces a thickened, T2-hyperintense ACL with preserved, continuous, taut fibers — the classic 'celery stalk' appearance — and no secondary signs of instability, exactly as described. An acute complete tear (A) shows fiber discontinuity and translation, which are absent here. A partial tear with retraction (B) would show some disrupted fibers; the hallmark of mucoid degeneration is that the fibers remain intact, which is the point trainees most often miss when they overcall a tear. A ganglion (D) is a discrete cyst, not diffuse intrasubstance striation, and does not by itself rupture fibers.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-2-post.stem: A 53-year-old golfer reports an aching knee with a sense of fullness on deep flexion but a stable Lachman test. MRI demonstrates an enlarged ACL of increased T2 signal in which the individual fascicles remain continuous and longitudinally oriented, with no bone bruise and a normal tibiofemoral relationship. Which interpretation is correct?

options.text: Complete mid-substance ACL tear with the empty-notch sign
Mucoid degeneration of the ACL, not a tear
High-grade partial ACL tear involving the anteromedial bundle
Acute interstitial ACL sprain with intrasubstance hemorrhage

knee-mri::prepost-kn-cruciates-2-post.explanation: Continuous, longitudinally oriented fibers with diffuse T2 signal (the 'celery stalk') and absent secondary signs in a clinically stable knee indicate mucoid degeneration rather than a tear. A complete tear (A) requires fiber discontinuity and an empty notch, which are absent. A high-grade partial tear (C) would disrupt some fascicles and often correlate with laxity; preserved continuity argues against it. An acute interstitial sprain (D) typically accompanies trauma and bone-marrow edema, which this chronic, atraumatic, bruise-free case lacks — the common error is mistaking benign mucoid signal for a tear.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-collateral-corner-1.stem: A 25-year-old skier has an acute valgus-and-rotation injury. MRI shows a curvilinear bony fragment avulsed from the lateral margin of the proximal tibia just below the joint line. Recognizing this finding should most prompt the radiologist to scrutinize which structure?

options.text: The medial patellofemoral ligament
The quadriceps tendon insertion
The pes anserinus tendons
The anterior cruciate ligament

knee-mri::prepost-kn-collateral-corner-1.explanation: The described fragment is a Segond fracture, an avulsion of the lateral capsular/anterolateral ligament complex that carries a very high association with ACL tears (and often meniscal injury); seeing it mandates a careful look at the ACL. The quadriceps tendon (B) and pes anserinus (C) are unrelated to this lateral capsular avulsion. The MPFL (A) is implicated in patellar dislocation, a different mechanism; trainees sometimes confuse the lateral Segond avulsion with patellofemoral injuries, but the Segond's key teaching point is its ACL association.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-collateral-corner-1.stem: A 28-year-old footballer sustained a pivoting injury. Coronal MRI reveals a thin vertically oriented cortical avulsion fragment along the lateral aspect of the proximal tibia at the site of the anterolateral capsular attachment. Identifying this fragment should raise the highest suspicion for concurrent injury of which structure?

options.text: The anterior cruciate ligament
The posterior cruciate ligament
The patellar tendon
The iliotibial band proximal origin

knee-mri::prepost-kn-collateral-corner-1.explanation: This is a Segond fracture — a lateral capsular avulsion — and its principal clinical significance is the strong association with an ACL tear (plus frequent meniscal tears), so the ACL is the priority for further evaluation. A PCL avulsion (B) is a posterior/medial mechanism, not this lateral fragment. The patellar tendon (C) attaches at the tibial tubercle anteriorly, not the lateral capsular margin. The ITB (D) is sometimes invoked because of its lateral location, but the avulsed structure here is the anterolateral capsule/ligament, whose recognized companion injury is the ACL.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-extensor-synovium-1.stem: A 58-year-old man could not extend his knee after stumbling. Sagittal MRI shows a full-thickness gap with fluid signal in the tendon immediately superior to the superior pole of the patella, and the patella sits abnormally low. Which diagnosis best fits these findings?

options.text: Quadriceps tendon rupture with patella baja
Patellar tendon rupture with patella alta
Quadriceps tendinosis without a tear
Patellar tendon rupture with patella baja

knee-mri::prepost-kn-extensor-synovium-1.explanation: A defect just above the superior patellar pole localizes to the quadriceps tendon; loss of the proximal extensor restraint lets the patellar tendon pull the patella inferiorly, producing patella baja. Patellar tendon rupture (B, D) occurs below the inferior pole and produces patella alta because the unopposed quadriceps retracts the patella superiorly — so 'patellar tendon with baja' (D) is internally inconsistent. Tendinosis (C) shows thickening and intermediate signal without a full-thickness fluid-filled gap and would not cause inability to extend; mixing up the tendon involved and the resulting patellar position is the classic trainee error.

knee-mri::prepost-kn-extensor-synovium-1.stem: A 34-year-old basketball player felt a pop while jumping and cannot actively straighten the knee. Sagittal MRI shows a full-thickness fluid-filled gap in the tendon just distal to the inferior pole of the patella, and the patella is positioned abnormally high. Which diagnosis best accounts for these findings?

options.text: Quadriceps tendon rupture with patella alta
Patellar tendinosis without a discrete tear
Patellar tendon rupture with patella alta
Quadriceps tendon rupture with patella baja

knee-mri::prepost-kn-extensor-synovium-1.explanation: A full-thickness gap just below the inferior patellar pole is a patellar tendon rupture; with the distal tether lost, the intact quadriceps retracts the patella upward, yielding patella alta. A quadriceps rupture (A, D) involves the tendon above the superior pole and produces patella baja, so 'quadriceps with alta' (A) is internally inconsistent. Patellar tendinosis (B) shows fusiform thickening with intermediate signal and intact fibers, not a fluid-filled discontinuity, and preserves active extension; confusing which tendon failed and the direction the patella migrates is the high-yield distinction being tested.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-orientation-sea.stem: A fellow reviewing a shoulder MRI wants to assess the subscapularis tendon at its lesser-tuberosity insertion and confirm that the long head of the biceps tendon is seated within the bicipital groove. Which imaging plane is best suited for evaluating both of these structures together?

options.text: Axial
Oblique coronal
Oblique sagittal
Abduction-external-rotation (ABER) oblique

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-orientation-sea.explanation: The axial plane displays the subscapularis tendon in cross-section at its lesser-tuberosity insertion and shows the long head of biceps within the bicipital groove, making it the workhorse plane for both. The oblique coronal plane is optimized for the supraspinatus and superior labrum and the oblique sagittal plane for muscle bulk and the cuff arch in profile, so choosing either mismatches a structure to a non-optimal plane; the ABER view is a problem-solving sequence for anteroinferior labral lesions, not a routine plane for the groove and subscapularis.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-orientation-sea.stem: During a systematic search, a fellow wants to grade fatty infiltration and muscle bulk of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus bellies (Goutallier/Warner assessment) on a shoulder MRI. Which plane is the standard reference for this evaluation?

options.text: Axial
Oblique coronal
Oblique sagittal
True (non-oblique) coronal

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-orientation-sea.explanation: The oblique sagittal plane (the 'Y' view, just medial to the glenoid) profiles the cuff muscle bellies within the supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae, which is where fatty infiltration and atrophy are graded. The oblique coronal plane best depicts the supraspinatus tendon and footprint rather than muscle bulk, the axial plane is optimized for the subscapularis and biceps groove, and a true coronal is not angled to the scapular plane, so selecting those confuses tendon/insertion or non-aligned planes with the muscle-belly plane.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-1-.stem: A 52-year-old with chronic shoulder pain has an MRI showing a thickened supraspinatus tendon with diffuse intermediate (not fluid-bright) signal on the fat-saturated T2 images, but no focal fluid-bright defect reaching either the articular or bursal surface. What does this finding most likely represent?

options.text: Articular-surface partial-thickness tear
Tendinosis (intermediate-signal degeneration)
Bursal-surface partial-thickness tear
Full-thickness tear with joint-to-bursa communication

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-1-.explanation: Tendon thickening with intermediate (not fluid-bright) signal that does not reach a surface is the classic appearance of tendinosis, reflecting myxoid/mucoid degeneration rather than a fiber gap. A partial tear requires focal fluid-bright signal reaching the articular or bursal surface, and a full-thickness tear requires a fluid-bright defect spanning the entire tendon with joint-to-bursa communication; calling intermediate non-surfacing signal a 'tear' is the most common over-reading error.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-1-.stem: A 60-year-old with weakness has an MRI showing a fluid-bright gap through the full thickness of the supraspinatus tendon, with high T2 signal continuous from the glenohumeral joint into the overlying subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation best fits this finding?

options.text: Tendinosis (intermediate-signal degeneration)
Intrasubstance partial-thickness tear
Articular-surface partial-thickness tear
Full-thickness tear with joint-to-bursa communication

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-1-.explanation: A fluid-bright defect spanning the entire tendon thickness with continuity of fluid signal between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa defines a full-thickness tear. Tendinosis shows intermediate, non-fluid signal without a gap, and an articular-surface or intrasubstance partial tear involves only part of the tendon thickness and does not create joint-to-bursa communication, so those choices underestimate a defect that clearly traverses the whole tendon.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-2-.stem: An overhead-throwing athlete has a partial supraspinatus tear. On the oblique coronal images, the fluid-bright defect reaches the deep (inferior) surface of the tendon at the footprint, while the superior (bursal) fibers remain intact. How is this partial tear best classified?

options.text: Bursal-sided tear
Intrasubstance (interstitial) tear
Articular-sided (PASTA) tear
Full-thickness tear

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-2-.explanation: A defect reaching the deep/articular surface at the footprint with intact bursal fibers is an articular-sided partial tear, the PASTA lesion (partial articular-sided supraspinatus tendon avulsion), which is the most common partial tear and typical in throwers. A bursal-sided tear reaches the superior surface, and an intrasubstance tear is confined within the tendon touching neither surface, so those answers misassign which surface the fluid contacts.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-2-.stem: A 48-year-old laborer with impingement symptoms has a partial supraspinatus tear. On fat-saturated images the fluid-bright defect reaches the superior surface of the tendon abutting the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa, while the deep articular fibers at the footprint remain intact. What is the correct classification?

options.text: Articular-sided (PASTA) tear
Bursal-sided tear
Intrasubstance (interstitial) tear
Full-thickness tear

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-2-.explanation: A partial tear whose fluid-bright defect reaches the superior surface adjacent to the subacromial bursa, with intact deep articular fibers, is a bursal-sided tear, classically associated with extrinsic impingement. An articular-sided (PASTA) tear contacts the deep surface instead, and an intrasubstance tear reaches neither surface; choosing those reverses or ignores which surface the defect contacts.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-3-.stem: A 67-year-old has a full-thickness supraspinatus tear. On the oblique sagittal images the supraspinatus muscle shows more fat than muscle (Goutallier grade 3-4), and on coronal images the tendon stump is retracted to the level of the glenoid. Which feature most strongly predicts poor reparability and should prompt earlier surgical discussion?

options.text: Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty infiltration with retraction to the glenoid
A small rim of reactive subacromial bursal fluid
Mild tendinosis of the adjacent infraspinatus
A type II (curved) acromion

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-3-.explanation: Advanced Goutallier 3-4 fatty infiltration combined with high-grade retraction (Patte stage III, to the glenoid) and muscle atrophy is largely irreversible and predicts poor healing and re-tear, so it argues for earlier surgical referral before further degeneration. Reactive bursal fluid, mild adjacent tendinosis, and acromial shape influence symptoms but are not the dominant reparability/prognosis drivers, so they are plausible but incorrect markers of irreparability.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-3-.stem: A 65-year-old has a full-thickness infraspinatus and supraspinatus tear. The surgeon asks which single imaging feature most strongly predicts that a durable repair is unlikely and warrants earlier surgical discussion. Which feature carries that prognostic weight?

options.text: A moderate subacromial-subdeltoid bursal effusion
A type III (hooked) acromion on the oblique sagittal images
Mild tendinosis of the intact subscapularis
Goutallier grade 3-4 fatty infiltration with muscle atrophy and retraction to the glenoid

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-rotator-cuff-3-.explanation: Advanced Goutallier 3-4 fatty infiltration with muscle atrophy and high-grade (Patte III) retraction to the glenoid reflects poor muscle-tendon quality that is largely irreversible and predicts low healing and high re-tear rates, so it drives earlier surgical discussion. A bursal effusion is nonspecific, a hooked acromion relates to impingement risk rather than tendon reparability, and mild subscapularis tendinosis is incidental, so each is a real but non-dominant finding that does not determine reparability.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-subscapularis-b.stem: A 55-year-old has an MRI showing a tear of the upper subscapularis tendon at the lesser tuberosity. On the axial images the long head of the biceps tendon is no longer within the bicipital groove and instead lies medially, displaced over the torn subscapularis. This medial biceps displacement is best interpreted as which of the following?

options.text: A normal variant biceps position seen with a shallow groove
An indirect sign of a biceps pulley lesion associated with the subscapularis tear
An isolated supraspinatus tear without pulley involvement
Mucoid degeneration of the intra-articular biceps tendon

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-subscapularis-b.explanation: The subscapularis tendon contributes to the medial wall of the biceps pulley/reflection, so an upper subscapularis tear frequently disrupts the pulley and allows the biceps to sublux medially out of the groove, a recognized indirect sign of subscapularis/pulley injury. A shallow groove alone does not displace a seated tendon over a torn subscapularis, supraspinatus tears do not explain medial biceps subluxation, and mucoid degeneration alters signal rather than position, so those distractors miss the pulley mechanism.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-subscapularis-b.stem: A fellow notices on axial images that the long head of the biceps tendon has subluxed medially and appears to lie deep to (medial to) the lesser tuberosity. Recognizing the anatomic relationship of the biceps pulley, which additional tendon should be scrutinized most closely for an associated tear?

options.text: Infraspinatus
Teres minor
Subscapularis
Supraspinatus

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-subscapularis-b.explanation: The subscapularis tendon forms the medial sling of the biceps pulley, so medial subluxation of the biceps out of the groove should trigger close evaluation of the subscapularis, which is commonly torn in this pattern. The infraspinatus and teres minor are posterior cuff and do not stabilize the biceps medially, and an isolated supraspinatus tear does not produce medial biceps subluxation, so those choices overlook the pulley's medial anatomy.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-labrum-slap-1-p.stem: A 24-year-old overhead athlete has an MR arthrogram for clicking and deep pain. At the superior labrum, contrast tracks into a cleft that is smooth, oriented medially, parallels the glenoid rim, and stops at the biceps anchor without lateral extension. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Type II SLAP tear
Anterosuperior sublabral foramen
Normal sublabral recess
Bucket-handle labral tear

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-labrum-slap-1-p.explanation: A sublabral recess is a normal variant: a smooth, medially oriented cleft that parallels the glenoid and stops at the biceps anchor without lateral extension or irregular margins, exactly as described. A type II SLAP tear would show contrast extending laterally into the labral substance with irregular margins posterior to the anchor, and a bucket-handle tear shows a displaced labral fragment; calling this smooth medial recess a tear is the most common over-reading error, while a sublabral foramen is an anterosuperior (not superior-at-the-anchor) variant.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-labrum-slap-1-p.stem: A 27-year-old pitcher has an MR arthrogram. At the superior labrum, gadolinium extends laterally into the labral substance with irregular margins and tracks posterior to the biceps anchor. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Normal sublabral recess
Type II SLAP tear
Normal sublabral foramen
Mucoid degeneration of the labrum

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-labrum-slap-1-p.explanation: Lateral extension into the labral substance, irregular margins, and tracking posterior to the biceps anchor distinguish a SLAP tear from a normal recess, which is smooth, medially oriented, and ends at the anchor. A sublabral recess and a sublabral foramen are both smooth variants that follow the glenoid rather than extending irregularly into the labrum, so labeling this finding either of those is the classic benign-variant pitfall; mucoid degeneration produces ill-defined intrasubstance signal without a discrete margin-irregular contrast-filled cleft.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-1-p.stem: A 30-year-old with recurrent anterior dislocations has an MR arthrogram. The anteroinferior labroligamentous complex is displaced medially and 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck along an intact but stripped scapular periosteum. Which lesion does this describe?

options.text: Bankart lesion, with a disrupted anterior scapular periosteum
ALPSA lesion, with the complex rolled up medially on an intact stripped periosteum
Perthes lesion, with an intact periosteum and a non-displaced labrum resynovialized in place
HAGL lesion, with avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament from the humerus

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-1-p.explanation: ALPSA (anterior labroligamentous periosteal sleeve avulsion) is medial displacement of the labroligamentous sleeve along an intact but stripped periosteum, classically chronic/recurrent with the labrum 'rolled up' on the glenoid neck. A Bankart has a torn periosteum so the labrum is not held medially, a Perthes keeps the periosteum intact but the labrum stays anatomically positioned rather than rolled up medially, and a HAGL is a humeral-sided ligament avulsion rather than a labral lesion.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-1-p.stem: A 22-year-old has anterior instability. On MR arthrogram the inferior glenohumeral ligament is avulsed from its humeral neck attachment, the axillary pouch takes on a 'J' configuration, and contrast leaks extra-articularly on the humeral side. The anteroinferior labrum is intact. Which lesion is this?

options.text: Bankart lesion, with anteroinferior labral avulsion from the glenoid
GLAD lesion, with a glenolabral articular cartilage defect and intact ligaments
Perthes lesion, with a non-displaced anteroinferior labral tear on an intact periosteum
HAGL lesion, with humeral avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament and a J-shaped axillary pouch
