shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-1-p.explanation: A HAGL lesion is humeral-sided avulsion of the inferior glenohumeral ligament, producing a 'J-sign' of the axillary pouch and an extra-articular contrast leak on the humeral side, with the glenoid labrum intact. A Bankart and a Perthes are glenoid/labral-sided lesions and a GLAD is a glenolabral cartilage injury, so all three represent the classic pitfall of fixating on the glenoid side while missing the humeral-sided ligament avulsion; none produces a humeral contrast leak or a J-shaped pouch.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-2-p.stem: A 26-year-old has had four anterior dislocations. MRI suggests substantial anterior glenoid bone loss and a Hill-Sachs lesion that appears to engage. The surgeon wants precise osseous quantification to decide between an arthroscopic soft-tissue repair and a Latarjet. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed to arthroscopic Bankart repair on clinical grounds alone
Obtain a radionuclide bone scan of the glenoid rim
Repeat MRI with intra-articular gadolinium to re-image the labrum
Obtain CT to quantify glenoid bone loss and the Hill-Sachs track

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-2-p.explanation: When MRI suggests significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20% or more) or an engaging/off-track Hill-Sachs, CT — often 3D with the humeral head digitally subtracted — best quantifies the osseous defect and drives the choice between soft-tissue repair and a bony procedure such as Latarjet. Proceeding to isolated Bankart repair ignores that high bone loss predicts soft-tissue-repair failure, a bone scan gives no quantification of bone loss, and an MR arthrogram characterizes soft tissue but underestimates osseous defects compared with CT.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-2-p.stem: A 19-year-old collision-sport athlete has recurrent anterior dislocations. CT shows ~25% anterior glenoid bone loss and an off-track Hill-Sachs lesion. Based on these osseous findings, which surgical strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Isolated arthroscopic Bankart repair, the labrum being the primary stabilizer
Nonoperative management with bracing and activity modification
A bony augmentation procedure such as Latarjet to restore the deficient glenoid arc
Arthroscopic capsular plication to retension the anterior capsule

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-instability-2-p.explanation: Critical glenoid bone loss (around 20-25% or more) with an off-track/engaging Hill-Sachs predicts high failure rates for soft-tissue repair, so a bony procedure such as Latarjet — which lengthens the glenoid arc and adds a sling effect — is favored. Isolated Bankart repair reflects the misconception that labral repair suffices despite bone loss, bracing wrongly assumes bone loss is irrelevant to recurrence, and capsular plication addresses soft tissue but does not restore the deficient bony track.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-capsule-adhesiv.stem: A 54-year-old with diabetes has months of progressive global stiffness and a marked loss of passive external rotation. Which MRI finding most specifically supports adhesive capsulitis rather than a rotator cuff tear?

options.text: Fluid-bright signal spanning the supraspinatus from articular to bursal surface
Thickening and edema of the coracohumeral ligament and rotator interval with obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle
Intermediate signal and thickening of the supraspinatus tendon without a surface defect
Fluid in the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-capsule-adhesiv.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis shows soft-tissue thickening and edema at the rotator interval and coracohumeral ligament with loss of the normal subcoracoid fat triangle, plus axillary pouch/inferior capsule thickening — findings that explain the global loss of motion. Fluid-bright transmural supraspinatus signal describes a full-thickness cuff tear (the mimic to be excluded), intermediate non-surfacing tendon signal describes tendinosis, and bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not indicate capsulitis.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-capsule-adhesiv.stem: A 49-year-old has 4 months of painful shoulder stiffness limiting motion in all planes. MRI is ordered to distinguish adhesive capsulitis from a cuff problem. Which constellation of findings is most specific for adhesive capsulitis?

options.text: Retraction of the supraspinatus to the glenoid with Goutallier grade 3 fatty infiltration
A laterally extending superior labral contrast cleft with irregular margins
Thickening and edema of the axillary pouch and inferior glenohumeral ligament with rotator-interval and coracohumeral ligament thickening
Isolated subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid without capsular changes

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-capsule-adhesiv.explanation: The specific MRI signature of adhesive capsulitis is thickening and edema of the axillary pouch/inferior glenohumeral ligament combined with rotator-interval and coracohumeral ligament thickening and obliteration of the subcoracoid fat triangle, often with capsular enhancement. Supraspinatus retraction with fatty infiltration describes a chronic cuff tear (the mimic), a laterally extending irregular superior labral cleft describes a SLAP tear, and isolated bursal fluid is nonspecific and does not establish capsulitis.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-nerve-entrapmen.stem: A 35-year-old volleyball player has posterior shoulder weakness. MRI shows a posterosuperior labral tear with a paralabral cyst extending into the spinoglenoid notch. Which additional finding best explains the weakness and should be reported?

options.text: Denervation of both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, from compression at the suprascapular notch
Denervation of the deltoid, from axillary nerve compression in the quadrilateral space
Denervation edema or fatty atrophy of the infraspinatus alone, from suprascapular nerve compression at the spinoglenoid notch
Denervation of the subscapularis, from upper subscapular nerve involvement

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-nerve-entrapmen.explanation: A cyst at the spinoglenoid notch compresses the suprascapular nerve after its branch to the supraspinatus has departed, so denervation is isolated to the infraspinatus (edema acutely, fatty atrophy chronically). Denervation of both muscles is the more proximal suprascapular-notch pattern, and deltoid or subscapularis denervation involves different nerves (axillary, subscapular) not affected by a spinoglenoid notch cyst; the teaching point is to look beyond the labrum to muscle denervation signal.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-nerve-entrapmen.stem: A 41-year-old has shoulder pain and weakness of both abduction and external rotation. MRI shows a paralabral cyst arising from a superior labral tear that extends medially into the suprascapular notch. Which pattern of muscle denervation do you most expect?

options.text: Isolated infraspinatus denervation, the cyst lying distal to the supraspinatus branch
Isolated teres minor denervation, from axillary nerve compression
Isolated supraspinatus denervation, the cyst sparing the infraspinatus branch
Denervation of both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, the cyst compressing the suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-nerve-entrapmen.explanation: A cyst at the suprascapular notch compresses the nerve proximal to both motor branches, so both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus show denervation, matching the combined abduction and external-rotation weakness. Isolated infraspinatus denervation is the more distal spinoglenoid-notch pattern, isolated supraspinatus denervation inverts the anatomy because a proximal lesion cannot spare the infraspinatus, and teres minor denervation involves a different nerve; the key is to localize the cyst along the nerve's course.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-postoperative-1.stem: Five months after supraspinatus repair, a patient has persistent pain. On fluid-sensitive images the repaired tendon shows intermediate-to-heterogeneous signal and mild thickening, with a little subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid and focal susceptibility artifact near the anchors. How should this be interpreted?

options.text: Recurrent full-thickness tear, because persistent intratendinous signal indicates failure
Recurrent full-thickness tear, because subacromial-subdeltoid bursal fluid confirms a gap
Recurrent full-thickness tear, because anchor susceptibility artifact represents a tendon defect
Expected postoperative change, because heterogeneous signal, mild bursal fluid, and anchor artifact are normal here

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-postoperative-1.explanation: Repaired cuff tendons normally remain thickened and heterogeneous with intermediate-to-increased signal for many months, and mild bursal fluid and anchor susceptibility artifact are expected, so none of these indicates failure. Options A, B, and C each reflect the common error of overcalling a recurrent tear from nonspecific postoperative signal, reactive bursal fluid, or hardware artifact; a recurrent full-thickness tear instead requires a fluid-bright gap spanning the tendon with fluid communication.

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-postoperative-1.stem: Seven months after rotator cuff repair, a patient has recurrent pain and weakness. On fluid-sensitive fat-saturated images there is a fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full thickness of the repaired tendon, with fluid communicating between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa. Which interpretation is best supported?

options.text: Recurrent full-thickness tear, because a fluid-bright gap spans the tendon with joint-to-bursa communication
Expected postoperative change, because some increased intratendinous signal is normal after repair
Expected postoperative change, because anchor susceptibility artifact mimics a defect
Tendinosis of the repaired tendon, because the signal is intermediate rather than fluid-bright

shoulder-mri::prepost-sh-postoperative-1.explanation: A fluid-bright (fluid-equivalent) defect spanning the full tendon thickness with fluid communication between the joint and the subacromial-subdeltoid bursa is the reliable sign of a recurrent full-thickness tear. Options B and C reflect the opposite error of dismissing a true fluid-bright gap as benign postoperative signal or artifact, and option D mischaracterizes the defect because the finding is fluid-equivalent, not the intermediate signal of tendinosis.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-anatomy-pre.stem: A 28-year-old has nonspecific anterior groin discomfort. MRI shows a smooth, linear high-signal cleft at the posteroinferior chondrolabral junction that follows the labral margin without displacement, blunting, or an adjacent paralabral cyst, and the labrum is otherwise normal in size and shape. What is the most appropriate management implication of this finding?

options.text: Refer for arthroscopic labral repair given a displaced bucket-handle tear
Order urgent aspiration for suspected paralabral cyst causing nerve compression
Treat as a likely normal sublabral sulcus variant; correlate clinically rather than refer for surgery
Escalate to oncology workup for a labral-based soft-tissue mass

hip-mri::prepost-hip-anatomy-pre.explanation: A smooth linear cleft following the labral margin without displacement, blunting, or a paralabral cyst is most consistent with a normal sublabral sulcus/recess variant and should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery. The location is important: sublabral sulci/recesses are most accepted as normal variants posteroinferiorly and at the anteroinferior junction, whereas a true cleft at the anterosuperior junction is the site where pathologic tears predominate and should not be casually dismissed as a variant. A bucket-handle tear (A) shows a displaced fragment, which is absent. There is no cyst to aspirate (B). A normal-shaped labrum with a thin cleft is not a mass (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-anatomy-post.stem: A 35-year-old recreational athlete has mild intermittent groin ache. MRI demonstrates a thin, regular high-signal line at the posteroinferior chondrolabral junction paralleling the labral base, with a triangular labrum of normal contour, no fragment displacement, and no surrounding cyst. How should this finding most appropriately direct management?

options.text: Diagnose a full-thickness radial labral tear and refer for reconstruction
Interpret as a normal sublabral recess; manage symptoms clinically without surgical referral
Recommend MR arthrography solely to drain an associated paralabral cyst
Initiate a marrow-replacement tumor protocol for a labral lesion

hip-mri::prepost-hip-anatomy-post.explanation: A thin regular line at the posteroinferior chondrolabral junction paralleling the labral base with a normally contoured labrum and no cyst or displacement indicates a normal sublabral recess variant; manage clinically. Location matters: a posteroinferior/inferior cleft is a recognized normal-variant site, in contrast to the anterosuperior junction where most true symptomatic tears occur and a cleft cannot be safely called a variant. A radial tear (A) disrupts and irregularly clefts the labrum, not seen here. There is no cyst to drain (C), and a normal-appearing labrum is not a tumor (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-stress-pre.stem: A 30-year-old marathoner has 2 weeks of groin pain. MRI shows marrow edema in the femoral neck with a linear T1-hypointense fracture line along the superolateral (tension) cortex extending partway across the neck. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Allow continued running with a 10% weekly mileage reduction and recheck in 6 weeks
Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral
Start protected weight-bearing with a cane and routine clinic follow-up in 3 months
Inject the hip with corticosteroid to control pain and resume training

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-stress-pre.explanation: A tension-side (superolateral) femoral neck stress fracture is at high risk of completion and displacement and is treated as a surgical emergency with non-weight-bearing and urgent referral. Continued running (A), partial weight-bearing with routine follow-up (C), or steroid injection (D) all risk catastrophic completion of the fracture.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-stress-post.stem: A 26-year-old triathlete reports 3 weeks of groin pain. MRI reveals femoral neck marrow edema with a short fracture line along the inferomedial (compression) cortex and no cortical break or displacement. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Immediate operative fixation that day as a surgical emergency
Continue full training with anti-inflammatories and no imaging follow-up
Protected weight-bearing with activity modification and close outpatient orthopedic follow-up
Same-day corticosteroid injection followed by return to competition

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-stress-post.explanation: A compression-side (inferomedial) femoral neck stress fracture without a cortical break is typically managed conservatively with protected weight-bearing, activity modification, and close follow-up. Emergent fixation (A) is reserved for tension-side or displaced fractures. Continuing full training (B) or steroid injection then competing (D) risk progression. The tension-vs-compression distinction is what changes management.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-occult-pre.stem: A 67-year-old woman with osteoporosis fell and has groin pain and inability to bear weight, but radiographs are read as negative. MRI shows an incomplete linear T1-hypointense fracture line with surrounding marrow edema in the femoral neck. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Discharge home with full weight-bearing since radiographs were negative
Provide a corticosteroid injection for presumed bursitis and mobilize
Schedule routine outpatient physiotherapy with weight-bearing as tolerated
Keep her non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic referral for an occult femoral neck fracture

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-occult-pre.explanation: An MRI-detected occult femoral neck fracture in an osteoporotic patient who cannot bear weight requires non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral because of the high risk of displacement. Discharging with full weight-bearing (A), steroid injection (B), or weight-bearing-as-tolerated physiotherapy (C) all risk fracture completion and displacement.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-occult-post.stem: An 82-year-old man fell from standing and has groin pain with inability to bear weight; two views of the hip are read as negative. MRI shows an incomplete T1-hypointense fracture line through the femoral neck with surrounding marrow edema and no displacement. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Keep him non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic referral for the occult femoral neck fracture
Discharge with weight-bearing as tolerated because the plain films were negative
Treat presumptively for greater trochanteric bursitis with a corticosteroid injection and mobilize
Arrange routine outpatient physiotherapy with a follow-up radiograph in 6 weeks

hip-mri::prepost-hip-bones-occult-post.explanation: An MRI-detected occult femoral neck fracture in an older adult who cannot bear weight needs non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral because of the high risk of completion and displacement, even when radiographs are negative. Weight-bearing as tolerated (B), a steroid injection for presumed bursitis (C), or routine delayed physiotherapy (D) all risk the fracture completing and displacing.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-cartilage-oa-pre.stem: A 48-year-old former athlete has groin pain. MRI shows DIFFUSE acetabular and femoral cartilage thinning with several subchondral cysts and global joint-space narrowing, rather than a single focal FAI rim lesion. The patient asks whether hip arthroscopy will fix the joint. Which interpretation best guides the management conversation?

options.text: Diffuse cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing indicates early/established OA, shifting management away from arthroscopy toward joint-preservation/arthroplasty pathways
The subchondral cysts represent an aggressive lytic tumor requiring biopsy before any treatment
The findings are a focal FAI rim injury that arthroscopic labral repair will durably correct
The pattern is transient bone marrow edema syndrome that will fully resolve with observation

hip-mri::prepost-hip-cartilage-oa-pre.explanation: Diffuse bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing reflects OA, which predicts poor arthroscopic outcomes and pushes the conversation toward preservation or arthroplasty pathways. Subchondral geodes in OA are not aggressive tumors (B). The diffuse pattern is not a focal FAI rim lesion amenable to arthroscopy (C). OA with structural cartilage loss is not self-limited transient edema (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-cartilage-oa-post.stem: A 52-year-old recreational cyclist has progressive groin pain. MRI demonstrates widespread bipolar cartilage thinning, multiple subchondral geodes, and superior joint-space narrowing, without a discrete focal rim lesion. The patient hopes arthroscopy will restore the joint. Which interpretation best frames the management discussion?

options.text: The geodes are an aggressive lytic process mandating urgent oncologic biopsy
This is an isolated focal chondral flap that arthroscopic debridement will permanently resolve
Diffuse bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing represents OA, moving care toward arthroplasty rather than arthroscopic labral surgery
This represents reversible transient marrow edema needing only rest

hip-mri::prepost-hip-cartilage-oa-post.explanation: Widespread bipolar cartilage loss with joint-space narrowing indicates OA; arthroscopy is poorly suited and the discussion shifts toward arthroplasty. OA geodes are not aggressive tumors (A). A diffuse process is not an isolated focal flap fixable by debridement (B), and structural OA cartilage loss is not reversible transient edema (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dont-miss-pre.stem: A 58-year-old man on chronic corticosteroids has 6 weeks of groin pain and a normal radiograph. MRI shows a serpentine subchondral line in the anterosuperior femoral head with a 'double-line sign' and preserved spherical head contour without subchondral collapse. What management does this finding most appropriately drive?

options.text: Reassure and discharge, since the head contour is preserved and no action is needed
Treat as routine transient marrow edema with observation and full activity
Refer to orthopedics for pre-collapse AVN, including protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving options
Start antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis based on the marrow signal

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dont-miss-pre.explanation: A double-line sign with a serpentine subchondral line in a steroid-exposed patient is osteonecrosis (AVN); identifying it before collapse opens a window for protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving procedures, so referral is key. Discharge (A) or treating as transient edema (B) misses a progressive disease. There is no infectious picture to justify antibiotics (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dont-miss-post.stem: A 44-year-old woman with a history of heavy alcohol use has 2 months of groin pain and normal radiographs. MRI demonstrates a serpiginous subchondral 'double-line sign' in the superior femoral head with maintained sphericity and no subchondral fracture or flattening. What management does this finding most appropriately direct?

options.text: Discharge with no follow-up because the femoral head remains spherical
Manage as benign transient bone marrow edema with unrestricted activity
Begin empiric IV antibiotics for suspected septic osteomyelitis
Orthopedic referral for pre-collapse osteonecrosis with protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving consideration

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dont-miss-post.explanation: A double-line sign with maintained sphericity in an at-risk patient indicates pre-collapse AVN; catching it before collapse allows protected weight-bearing and joint-preserving surgery, so referral is appropriate. Discharge (A) or calling it transient edema (B) misses progressive necrosis. No infectious features justify antibiotics (C).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dontmiss-scfe-pre.stem: A 13-year-old boy with obesity has 3 weeks of dull groin and knee pain and now walks with the foot externally rotated. MRI shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial slip of the epiphysis relative to the metaphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Make him non-weight-bearing and arrange urgent orthopedic surgical referral
Begin outpatient physical therapy and recheck in 6 weeks
Allow full activity with NSAIDs and clinic follow-up in 3 months
Inject the hip with corticosteroid and resume sports

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dontmiss-scfe-pre.explanation: Physeal widening with posteromedial epiphyseal slip is a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), which is an urgent, non-weight-bearing diagnosis requiring prompt surgical fixation to prevent further slip and AVN. Physical therapy (B), full activity (C), or steroid injection with return to sport (D) all risk progression and avascular necrosis.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dontmiss-scfe-post.stem: An 11-year-old boy with obesity has several weeks of vague knee and thigh pain and now limps with the leg turned outward. MRI of the hips shows widening and irregularity of the proximal femoral physis with posteromedial displacement of the epiphysis. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Begin a knee-focused physiotherapy program and reassess in 6 weeks
Allow unrestricted activity with NSAIDs and routine follow-up in 3 months
Make him non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedic surgical referral
Aspirate the knee to evaluate the referred knee pain

hip-mri::prepost-hip-dontmiss-scfe-post.explanation: Physeal widening with posteromedial epiphyseal displacement is a slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE); referred knee and thigh pain is classic, and the diagnosis is missed when attention stays on the knee. It requires non-weight-bearing and prompt surgical fixation to prevent further slip and avascular necrosis. Knee physiotherapy (A), unrestricted activity (B), or knee aspiration (D) all delay fixation and risk progression and AVN.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-labrum-pre.stem: A 25-year-old hockey player has activity-related anterior groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 68 degrees on the oblique-axial neck plane, an anterosuperior labral tear, AND adjacent acetabular rim cartilage delamination. Conservative rehab has failed over 4 months. What does the imaging add that most supports operative referral over continued rehab?

options.text: The 68-degree alpha angle alone proves symptomatic disease and mandates surgery regardless of cartilage
The rim chondral delamination accompanying cam morphology and a labral tear weights toward arthroscopic referral
The labral tear by itself indicates urgent same-week surgery to prevent dislocation
The findings indicate diffuse end-stage OA, so arthroplasty rather than arthroscopy is appropriate

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-labrum-pre.explanation: Associated rim chondral injury/delamination alongside cam morphology and a labral tear is a feature that shifts decision-making toward arthroscopic surgery, especially after failed rehab. Cam morphology is common and often asymptomatic, so the alpha angle alone is not dispositive (A). A labral tear is not a dislocation emergency (C). Focal rim delamination is not diffuse end-stage OA requiring arthroplasty (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-labrum-post.stem: A 29-year-old dancer has deep flexion-provoked groin pain. Dedicated hip MRI shows an alpha angle of 64 degrees, an anterosuperior labral tear, and focal full-thickness acetabular rim cartilage loss with delamination at the same location, after 5 months of failed physiotherapy. Which imaging feature most strongly supports proceeding to arthroscopic referral rather than further conservative care?

options.text: The 64-degree alpha angle alone confirms the pathology is symptomatic and dictates surgery
The isolated labral tear mandates emergent operative repair within days
The combination indicates global joint-space collapse warranting total hip arthroplasty
The focal rim chondral delamination co-located with cam morphology and a labral tear favors surgical referral

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-labrum-post.explanation: Co-located rim chondral delamination with cam morphology and a labral tear, after failed conservative care, weights toward arthroscopic referral. The alpha angle alone does not establish symptomatic disease because cam morphology is frequently asymptomatic (A). A labral tear is not an emergency (B). Focal rim loss is not global collapse requiring arthroplasty (C).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-cam-pre.stem: A 27-year-old with anterior groin pain has a dedicated hip MRI. On the oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck, the alpha angle measures 70 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and cartilage are intact and the patient is currently asymptomatic at rest. How should this isolated finding influence management?

options.text: Treat cam morphology as a common, often asymptomatic finding and correlate clinically rather than refer for surgery on imaging alone
Refer for prophylactic arthroscopic osteoplasty because the alpha angle exceeds 55 degrees
Order an urgent oncologic workup for the femoral neck osseous bump
Place the patient non-weight-bearing for an impending femoral neck fracture

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-cam-pre.explanation: Cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees) is common and frequently asymptomatic; isolated morphology without chondrolabral injury or concordant symptoms should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery. Prophylactic osteoplasty on morphology alone (B) is not indicated. A cam bump is not a tumor (C) or a fracture (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-cam-post.stem: A 32-year-old recreational swimmer has a dedicated hip MRI for lateral pain later attributed to the abductors. Incidentally, on the oblique-axial neck plane the alpha angle measures 66 degrees with an anterosuperior osseous bump, but the labrum and chondral surfaces are intact and there is no impingement-type pain with deep flexion. How should this isolated cam finding influence management?

options.text: Refer for prophylactic arthroscopic osteoplasty because the alpha angle is above 55 degrees
Treat cam morphology as a common, frequently asymptomatic finding and correlate clinically rather than operate on imaging alone
Order a staging workup for an aggressive osseous lesion of the femoral neck
Place the patient non-weight-bearing for an impending femoral neck fracture

hip-mri::prepost-hip-fai-cam-post.explanation: Cam morphology (alpha angle >55 degrees) is common and frequently asymptomatic; an isolated bump without chondrolabral injury or concordant impingement symptoms should be correlated clinically, not taken to surgery — especially when found incidentally. Prophylactic osteoplasty on morphology alone (A) is not indicated; a cam bump is neither an aggressive osseous lesion (C) nor a fracture (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-basics-pre.stem: A 24-year-old runner has deep anterior groin pain with pivoting. The ordered study is a large-FOV pelvis MRI read as 'no fracture, mild bursitis.' You suspect cam-type FAI and want to measure the alpha angle reliably. Which acquisition change most directly enables an accurate alpha-angle measurement to guide the FAI workup?

options.text: Add a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol with an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck
Repeat the large-FOV pelvis sequence with thinner coronal slices only
Add IV gadolinium to the existing large-FOV pelvis sequences
Switch the large-FOV T1 sequences to STIR for better fat suppression

hip-mri::prepost-hip-basics-pre.explanation: Alpha angle requires an oblique-axial plane prescribed along the femoral neck on a dedicated small-FOV hip study; the large-FOV pelvis screen lacks the resolution and plane to measure cam morphology. Thinner coronal slices (B) still do not give the neck-axis plane needed. IV gadolinium (C) does not address geometry/resolution. STIR (D) changes contrast, not the plane or FOV needed for the measurement.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-basics-post.stem: A 31-year-old soccer player has anterior hip pain catching with deep flexion. Outside imaging is a large-FOV pelvis MRI labeled 'unremarkable hips.' You want to confirm or exclude cam morphology before deciding on a surgical referral. Which protocol adjustment most directly provides the information needed to measure cam morphology?

options.text: Order a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol including an oblique-axial plane along the femoral neck
Re-run the large-FOV axial sequence with a larger matrix and the same coverage
Convert the large-FOV PD sequences to chemically fat-saturated images
Add post-contrast large-FOV imaging through both hips

hip-mri::prepost-hip-basics-post.explanation: Cam morphology and the alpha angle are measured on a small-FOV dedicated hip protocol with an oblique-axial plane aligned to the femoral neck. A larger matrix on the large-FOV axial (B) still does not provide the neck-axis plane. Chemical fat-sat (C) changes contrast, not the plane/FOV. Contrast (D) adds nothing for measuring osseous cam shape.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-search-pre.stem: A 40-year-old distance runner has worsening anterior groin pain, now with night pain and pain on weight-bearing. The reading radiologist anchors on an incidental small labral fray and stops. Given a fixed search-pattern discipline, which additional structure must be deliberately checked before translating the read into a return-to-run plan?

options.text: The tension (superolateral) side of the femoral neck for a stress fracture line and marrow edema
The contralateral asymptomatic acetabular labrum for incidental fraying
The trochanteric bursa for fluid before scanning the femoral neck
The skin and subcutaneous fat for postoperative seroma

hip-mri::prepost-hip-search-pre.explanation: Satisfaction-of-search after spotting a minor labral fray risks missing a superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck stress fracture, which is a surgical-urgency finding that changes a runner from 'return to run' to non-weight-bearing and referral. The asymptomatic contralateral labrum (B), bursa first (C), or skin (D) do not carry the same management-changing red-flag weight in this presentation.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-search-post.stem: A 22-year-old military recruit has 3 weeks of escalating groin pain after ramping up marching, now with rest pain. The MRI report fixates on a small herniation pit and concludes 'benign.' Applying a disciplined search pattern, which area most needs deliberate inspection before clearing the recruit to continue training?

options.text: The opposite hip's herniation pit for symmetry
The iliopsoas bursa for distension before assessing marrow
The gluteal subcutaneous tissues for cellulitis
The superolateral femoral neck for a tension-side stress fracture and reactive marrow edema

hip-mri::prepost-hip-search-post.explanation: Anchoring on a benign herniation pit can mask a tension-side (superolateral) femoral neck stress fracture in a high-risk recruit; finding it converts management to non-weight-bearing and urgent surgical referral. The contralateral pit (A), bursa first (B), or subcutaneous tissue (C) do not change this high-stakes decision the way the tension-side neck does.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-pre.stem: A 70-year-old woman has chronic lateral hip pain, a limp, and weak abduction. MRI shows a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus medius tendon tear with 2 cm of tendon retraction and fatty muscle atrophy at the greater trochanter. Prior corticosteroid injection and physiotherapy failed. What does this imaging most appropriately direct?

options.text: Another image-guided peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection as definitive treatment
Reassurance that this is simple trochanteric bursitis managed with stretching
Urgent escalation for suspected septic arthritis of the hip
Orthopedic surgical referral for consideration of abductor tendon repair

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-pre.explanation: A full-thickness, retracted gluteus medius tear with atrophy that has failed conservative care warrants surgical referral for possible tendon repair. Repeat steroid injection (A) does not address a retracted full-thickness tear and may worsen tendon quality. This is not simple bursitis (B). There is no septic picture (C).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-post.stem: A 64-year-old woman has persistent lateral hip pain and a Trendelenburg gait. MRI reveals a FULL-THICKNESS gluteus minimus and medius tendon tear with retraction and grade 2 fatty atrophy, after 6 months of physiotherapy and one peritrochanteric injection. What management does this imaging most appropriately support?

options.text: Repeat peritrochanteric corticosteroid injection as the definitive next step
Referral to orthopedic surgery to evaluate for abductor tendon reconstruction
Labeling the process as greater trochanteric bursitis treated with a home program
Emergent admission for presumed osteomyelitis of the trochanter

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-post.explanation: A retracted full-thickness abductor tendon tear with fatty atrophy refractory to conservative measures should prompt surgical referral for possible reconstruction. Another injection (A) will not restore a retracted tendon. This is more than bursitis (C). No infectious findings are described to warrant osteomyelitis admission (D).

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-hamstring-p.stem: A 45-year-old water-skier had a sudden hamstring 'pop' while the hip flexed and the knee extended. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed from the ischial tuberosity with 3 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Provide a home stretching program and clear for return to sport in 2 weeks
Refer promptly to orthopedic surgery for operative repair of the complete retracted avulsion
Provide an image-guided corticosteroid injection at the ischial tuberosity
Reassure as a grade 1 strain and continue normal activity

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-hamstring-p.explanation: A complete three-tendon proximal hamstring avulsion with significant (3 cm) retraction is a surgical lesion; timely referral is favored because the window for primary repair is best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring. A stretching program with rapid return (A) or calling it a grade 1 strain (D) understages a complete avulsion. A corticosteroid injection (C) does not address tendon retraction and may impair healing.

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-hamstring-p.stem: A 41-year-old man slipped on a boat ramp with the hip suddenly flexed and the knee held straight, feeling a painful pop in the buttock. MRI shows all three proximal hamstring tendons avulsed off the ischial tuberosity with 4 cm of retraction and a surrounding hematoma. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Refer promptly to orthopedic surgery for operative repair of the complete retracted avulsion
Prescribe a home eccentric-stretching program and clear for sport in 2 weeks
Inject the ischial tuberosity with corticosteroid to control pain
Reassure that this is a low-grade strain and continue normal activity

hip-mri::prepost-hip-tendons-hamstring-p.explanation: A complete three-tendon proximal hamstring avulsion with significant (4 cm) retraction is a surgical lesion; prompt referral is favored because primary-repair outcomes are best within a few weeks before further retraction and scarring. A stretching program with early return (B) or calling it a low-grade strain (D) understages a complete avulsion. A corticosteroid injection (C) does not address tendon retraction and can impair healing.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-protocol-pre.stem: A 19-year-old collegiate baseball pitcher reports medial elbow pain at ball release and loss of velocity over the season. On the moving-valgus exam he is most tender through the late-cocking arc, and you suspect a partial undersurface tear of the anterior bundle of the UCL. A non-contrast 3T elbow MRI shows periligamentous edema but no clear surface-reaching defect, and the read is called equivocal. Which imaging step is most appropriate to answer the clinical question?

options.text: MR arthrography of the elbow to look for contrast undercutting the sublime-tubercle attachment beyond the cartilage edge (the T-sign)
Repeat non-contrast MRI in a dedicated FABS (flexion-abduction-supination) position to grade the UCL
Routine elbow MR arthrography in an ABER position as the validated standard for every suspected UCL tear
No further imaging is needed; UCL partial tears are diagnosed on the common-flexor origin alone

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-protocol-pre.explanation: The clinical question is a suspected partial undersurface UCL tear after an equivocal non-contrast study, so the next step is MR arthrography: intra-articular contrast undercuts the sublime-tubercle attachment and reveals the T-sign (fluid/contrast tracking BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge while proximal fibers stay attached), and it is the most sensitive study for the undersurface tear. B is wrong because FABS is a dedicated DISTAL BICEPS footprint view (partial-vs-complete grading and retraction), not a UCL test. C is wrong because there is no validated routine elbow 'ABER' arthrogram; the elbow-specific evidence base for valgus/abduction positioning adjuncts is thin (unlike the shoulder ABER), and arthrography is added selectively, not for every suspected UCL tear. D is wrong because the common-flexor origin overlies but does not substitute for the UCL; you must image the ligament itself, and a partial undersurface tear is exactly what contrast is needed to confirm.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-protocol-post.stem: A 47-year-old man felt a tearing pop in the antecubital fossa while lowering a heavy box and now has a painful, bruised proximal arm. On exam the hook test is difficult to interpret because of swelling, and you cannot tell whether the distal biceps tendon is partially or completely torn or how far any complete tear has retracted, which will determine whether the surgeon can do a direct anatomic repair. Which imaging approach most directly answers that question?

options.text: Routine elbow MR arthrography, since intra-articular contrast is required to evaluate any suspected tendon tear
A coronal-only protocol, because the distal biceps and its retraction are best assessed on the coronal plane
MRI including a dedicated FABS (flexion-abduction-supination) view to lay the radial-tuberosity footprint flat for partial-vs-complete grading and retraction
MR arthrography in an elbow ABER position, the validated routine for distal biceps tears

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-protocol-post.explanation: The question is distal biceps partial-vs-complete plus retraction, and the FABS (flexion-abduction-supination, prone) view is the dedicated technique that lays the bifurcated radial-tuberosity footprint flat for partial-tear grading and for measuring how far a complete tear has retracted (also noting lacertus fibrosus status, which tethers the stump and can mask a complete tear). A is wrong because the distal biceps is an extra-articular structure best seen on non-contrast fluid-sensitive imaging; arthrography is reserved for intra-articular questions (T-sign, loose bodies, OCD stability) and is not required for a tendon tear. B is wrong because the distal biceps and its retraction are read on the SAGITTAL plane (and FABS), not coronal alone; coronal is the UCL/T-sign plane. D is wrong because there is no validated routine elbow 'ABER' arthrogram for the distal biceps; that conflates a shoulder positioning maneuver and the wrong contrast indication.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-search-pre.stem: A 46-year-old recreational tennis player has 5 months of refractory lateral elbow pain that worsened after a corticosteroid injection, plus a vague sense of the elbow 'giving way' when pushing up from a chair. Coronal T2 FS MRI shows a thickened, intermediate-to-fluid-bright common extensor origin with a deep, full-thickness gap at the lateral epicondyle that undercuts toward the radiocapitellar joint. The reading focuses on the extensor tendon and is about to sign the case as lateral epicondylitis. Which interpretation most appropriately completes the linked search and directs management?

options.text: The full-thickness common-extensor gap is the entire lesion; report lateral epicondylitis and manage with rehab, since a tendon read does not require tracing the adjacent ligament

Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest, because a deep extensor tear undercutting the radiocapitellar joint can take the LUCL origin and produce posterolateral rotatory instability that changes management to ligament repair/reconstruction
Reactive lateral-epicondyle marrow edema accompanying the tendinosis is itself diagnostic of an LCL tear, so refer for reconstruction on that basis
Read the tendons before the bones and ligaments and call the case complete once the extensor tendon is graded, because the mechanical symptoms reflect simple tendinopathy

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-search-pre.explanation: This is a satisfaction-of-search trap: after an obvious lateral epicondylitis you must complete the linked lateral search and trace the LUCL to the supinator crest. The LUCL/RCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon, so a deep, full-thickness extensor gap that undercuts/communicates with the radiocapitellar joint can take the LUCL and produce posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI) — a surgical problem and a recognized complication of prior lateral steroid injection/release, fitting the 'giving way' history. That escalates from rehab to ligament repair/reconstruction, making B correct. A stops at the tendinopathy and misses the LUCL → PLRI — the named misconception. C over-calls reactive epicondyle marrow edema (and thin RCL signal) as an LCL tear, which the reference explicitly warns against; edema alone does not equal a ligament tear. D endorses reading tendons before bones/ligaments and signing off early — the exact habit the search pattern (bones/marrow and ligaments before satisfaction-of-search on a tendon) is built to prevent, and it ignores the instability symptoms.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-search-post.stem: A 24-year-old man is referred after an elbow dislocation that was reduced on the field; he now has recurrent instability with the forearm in varus and feels the elbow shift when bearing weight on a flexed arm. MRI/CT shows a torn LUCL at its humeral origin and a small fracture of the anteromedial facet of the coronoid; the radial head and the rest of the coronoid are intact, and the fragment measures only a few millimeters. The interpreting clinician notes the coronoid fragment is small and is inclined to treat it as a stable injury. Which interpretation most appropriately completes the post-dislocation search and directs management?

options.text: Because the anteromedial facet fragment is only a few millimeters, it is mechanically stable and the elbow can be managed nonoperatively with early motion
The LUCL tear is the only relevant lesion; the small coronoid fragment can be ignored once the lateral ligament is documented
Reading the ligaments before the bones is acceptable here, so the coronoid does not need a dedicated review once the LUCL is called
Despite its small size, the anteromedial coronoid facet fracture with the LUCL injury indicates varus posteromedial rotatory instability and is surgical, so it must be reported and referred

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-search-post.explanation: In any post-dislocation elbow the read is a circle — LCL/LUCL, then the coronoid (including the anteromedial facet), then the radial head — and a small fragment does not mean a stable elbow. The anteromedial coronoid facet fracture looks small on imaging but, combined with an LUCL injury, defines varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI); it is highly unstable, surgical, and drives early arthrosis if missed, making D correct. A is the named misconception — judging the fragment stable because it looks small — when the anteromedial facet is precisely the small-but-unstable, surgical lesion. B stops at the LUCL and drops the coronoid, the satisfaction-of-search miss this concept targets. C endorses skipping a dedicated coronoid review after calling the ligament; the disciplined search reviews the bones/coronoid in every post-dislocation elbow rather than stopping once one structure is named.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ocd-stability.stem: A 15-year-old competitive baseball pitcher has 3 months of lateral elbow pain and catching. Coronal and sagittal fat-suppressed T2 images show a focal anterolateral capitellar osteochondral lesion. A high-signal cartilage cleft extends from the articular surface down to the fragment interface, and the fragment is no longer congruent with the parent bone. There is no joint-fluid-signal line completely undercutting the fragment, but surrounding marrow edema is present. Which interpretation most appropriately drives management?

options.text: The surrounding marrow edema is itself the instability sign, so this lesion is unstable on that basis and surgery is indicated
This is Panner disease, which is the same entity as capitellar OCD at this age and resolves with rest alone
The cartilage cleft reaching the fragment with loss of congruity makes this an unstable lesion; stop throwing and refer for surgical evaluation
Because no subchondral cyst measures at least 5 mm, the validated elbow criterion for instability is not met and continued throwing is appropriate

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ocd-stability.explanation: Instability is judged by whether the fragment is mechanically compromised. A high-signal cartilage cleft (delamination) reaching the fragment is one of the recognized unstable signs — along with a fluid-bright line completely undercutting the fragment, a sizeable or multiple subchondral cysts, and a displaced fragment/loose body — and here the lesion has also lost congruity, so the correct action is to stop throwing and refer (C). Surrounding marrow edema alone is NOT a stability sign; it appears in both stable and unstable lesions, so it cannot by itself classify the lesion as unstable (A). Panner disease and OCD are different entities — Panner is an osteochondrosis of the entire ossific nucleus in a younger child (~5–10 yr) with no fragment or cyst, whereas this focal adolescent fragmenting lesion is OCD; they are not the same (B). The 5 mm cyst figure is a knee-derived (De Smet) criterion not validated for the capitellum, so the absence of a 5 mm cyst neither rules out instability nor justifies continued throwing — and a cartilage cleft reaching the fragment is already an unstable sign here regardless of cyst size (D).

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ocd-stability.stem: A 14-year-old gymnast has lateral elbow pain. Coronal and sagittal fat-suppressed T2 images show a focal capitellar osteochondral lesion with a continuous joint-fluid-signal line tracking completely beneath the fragment, separating it from the parent bone. The overlying cartilage and the demarcating margin are otherwise present, and there is mild surrounding marrow edema. Which interpretation most appropriately drives management?

options.text: A fluid-bright line completely undercutting the fragment makes this an unstable lesion; stop the aggravating activity and refer for surgical evaluation
The surrounding marrow edema is the determining instability sign, so the fragment is unstable purely on that basis
Any isolated high-T2 line at the interface is definitely an unstable lesion even without a cartilage breach or cyst, so this is unstable for that reason
This is Panner disease rather than OCD, so it will remodel with rest and needs no stability assessment

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ocd-stability.explanation: A T2 fluid-bright line that completely undercuts the fragment and matches joint-fluid signal is the most reliable unstable sign in capitellar OCD — it indicates the fragment is no longer fixed to the parent bone — so the correct action is to stop the aggravating activity and refer (A). Surrounding marrow edema alone is NOT a stability criterion because it is present in both stable and unstable lesions; it cannot determine instability by itself (B). An ISOLATED high-T2 line without a cartilage breach or cyst is actually INDETERMINATE (it can be healing granulation tissue), not automatically unstable — the distinction here is that this line fully undercuts the fragment, which is what makes it unstable (C). Panner disease is a different entity from OCD — an osteochondrosis of the whole ossific nucleus in a younger child (~5–10 yr) with no fragment or undercutting fluid line — so calling this focal undercut fragment Panner and skipping the stability assessment is incorrect (D).

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-tsign-pre.stem: A 19-year-old college pitcher has medial elbow pain late in his throwing motion. Coronal T2 fat-saturated MR arthrography shows fluid/contrast tracking medially along the sublime tubercle and undercutting the distal attachment of the UCL anterior bundle BEYOND the edge of the articular cartilage, while the proximal fibers remain attached, forming an inverted 'T' with the joint-fluid column. There is no full-thickness gap or medial extravasation. The moving-valgus test reproduces his pain. How should this finding most appropriately direct management?

options.text: The contrast tracking only a couple of millimetres under the most distal fibers is the expected normal distal synovial recess, so no UCL injury should be reported
Because distal UCL tears definitively outnumber proximal tears, the location alone confirms a full-thickness tear and mandates immediate Tommy John reconstruction
The partial-thickness MRI grade by itself dictates surgery, so proceed directly to reconstruction regardless of the athlete's demand or the moving-valgus exam
This is the T-sign of a partial undersurface (distal) UCL tear; manage by integrating the lesion features, the athlete's demand, and the moving-valgus exam rather than by the MRI grade alone

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-tsign-pre.explanation: Contrast undercutting the distal UCL attachment BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge while proximal fibers stay attached is the T-sign of a partial-thickness undersurface (articular-sided, distal) tear — the classic throwing injury, best shown on MR arthrography. The correct management integrates lesion features (location/surface, acute vs chronic), the athlete's demand, and the moving-valgus exam, not the MRI grade alone. Option A is the named recess trap: a couple of millimetres of undercutting can be a normal synovial recess, but here the fluid extends BEYOND the cartilage edge, which is the abnormal threshold — so this is not simply a normal recess. Option B is false on two counts: distal-versus-proximal predominance is genuinely not uniform in the literature (proximal partials are at least as common in several series), and an undersurface T-sign with intact proximal fibers is partial, not full-thickness, so it does not mandate reconstruction. Option C misstates management: no single MRI cutoff drives the decision — surgeons weight location, surface, chronicity, demand, and the moving-valgus exam, and most low-grade partials in throwers are managed nonoperatively first.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-tsign-pos.stem: A 24-year-old javelin thrower has medial elbow pain at release. Coronal fluid-sensitive fat-saturated MRI (with intra-articular contrast) shows high-signal fluid undermining the sublime-tubercle insertion of the UCL anterior bundle and extending PAST the articular-cartilage margin, while the more proximal fibers stay attached and intact; no full-thickness defect or contrast extravasation is seen. Her moving-valgus test is positive in the painful arc. Which interpretation and management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: The few millimetres of fluid under the most distal fibers represent the normal distal synovial recess, so the UCL should be reported as intact
This is the T-sign of a partial undersurface (distal) UCL tear; base management on the lesion features together with the athlete's demand and the moving-valgus exam, not on the MRI grade alone
Since distal tears definitively predominate over proximal tears, the distal location establishes a full-thickness tear and routine ulnar-nerve transposition should accompany the reconstruction
The partial-thickness grade on MRI alone determines the operation, so refer for reconstruction independent of her throwing demand or the moving-valgus exam

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-tsign-pos.explanation: Fluid undermining the distal/sublime-tubercle UCL attachment and extending BEYOND the articular-cartilage margin with intact proximal fibers is the T-sign of a partial-thickness undersurface (distal) tear. Management is driven by the lesion (location/surface, acute vs chronic), the athlete's demand, and the moving-valgus exam — not by the MRI grade in isolation. Option A is the recess trap: a sliver of undercutting can be a normal synovial recess, but fluid extending PAST the cartilage edge is the abnormal threshold, so this is a tear, not a normal recess. Option C is wrong twice over: distal-versus-proximal predominance is not uniform in the literature (it is not true that distal tears definitively predominate), an undersurface T-sign with attached proximal fibers is partial rather than full-thickness, and ulnar-nerve transposition with UCL reconstruction is SELECTIVE (for preoperative ulnar symptoms or instability), not routine. Option D misstates management: no single MRI grade dictates the operation — location, surface, chronicity, demand, and the moving-valgus exam together drive it, and most low-grade partials in throwers are treated nonoperatively first.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-lucl-plri-pre.stem: A 24-year-old rock climber sustained a simple posterolateral elbow dislocation that was reduced on the field and is now concentrically reduced and stable on exam. MRI shows an avulsed lateral ulnar collateral ligament (LUCL) at the lateral epicondyle with intact medial structures, and a separate small anteromedial coronoid facet fragment with surrounding marrow edema. Which interpretation most accurately frames the instability and surgical risk in this elbow?

options.text: The LUCL injury threatens posterolateral rotatory instability, while the anteromedial coronoid facet fracture is a distinct varus posteromedial rotatory instability lesion that is surgical despite its small size
The LUCL tear and the anteromedial coronoid facet fracture reflect the same single instability mechanism, so naming one is sufficient for the report
The anteromedial coronoid facet fragment is small and therefore mechanically stable, so it can be left out of the impression
Because the elbow dislocated, the primary lesion is a torn medial (ulnar) collateral ligament, and valgus is the only post-traumatic instability to address

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-lucl-plri-pre.explanation: The LUCL (lateral epicondyle → posterior to the radial head → supinator crest) is the principal restraint to posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI), so its avulsion must be flagged in any post-dislocation elbow. The anteromedial coronoid facet fracture is a SEPARATE, distinct mechanism — varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI) — and although the fragment looks small on radiographs it is highly unstable and surgical, with untreated VPMRI driving early arthrosis; A captures both correctly. B is wrong because PLRI and VPMRI are different mechanisms, not one lesion. C is wrong because at the coronoid 'small does not mean stable' — the anteromedial facet fragment is the classic small-but-unstable surgical lesion. D inverts the O'Driscoll circle: a simple dislocation disrupts soft tissue lateral-to-medial (LUCL → capsule → MCL), so the LCL — not the MCL — is the primary lesion in most simple dislocations, and valgus is not the only post-traumatic instability.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-lucl-plri-pos.stem: A 31-year-old laborer is referred 8 weeks after a simple elbow dislocation with recurrent painful clicking and a positive lateral pivot-shift apprehension sign. MRI demonstrates an attenuated, surface-disrupted lateral ulnar collateral ligament arcing from the lateral epicondyle toward the supinator crest, plus a small anteromedial coronoid facet fracture. The medial (ulnar) collateral ligament is intact. Which interpretation best characterizes the two findings and their surgical relevance?

options.text: Because the elbow dislocated, the dominant lesion is an ulnar collateral ligament tear and valgus is the only meaningful post-traumatic instability
The anteromedial coronoid facet fragment is small, so it is stable and need not change management

The lateral ulnar collateral ligament injury underlies posterolateral rotatory instability, and the anteromedial coronoid facet fracture is a separate varus posteromedial rotatory instability lesion that is surgical even though it is small
The lateral ulnar collateral ligament tear and the anteromedial coronoid facet fracture are simply two expressions of one and the same instability mechanism

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-lucl-plri-pos.explanation: The LUCL (lateral epicondyle → posterior to the radial head → supinator crest) is the key restraint to posterolateral rotatory instability (PLRI), which fits this patient's recurrent clicking and positive pivot-shift; its competence must be commented on in every post-dislocation elbow. The anteromedial coronoid facet fracture is a DISTINCT lesion of varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI) — small on radiographs but highly unstable and surgical — so C is correct. A inverts the O'Driscoll circle: simple dislocations disrupt lateral-to-medial (LUCL → capsule → MCL), making the LCL, not the MCL, the primary lesion, so valgus is not the only post-traumatic instability. B is wrong because at the coronoid 'small does not mean stable' — the anteromedial facet fragment is the classic small-but-unstable surgical fracture. D is wrong because PLRI and VPMRI are two separate mechanisms, not one.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-biceps-lacert.stem: A 47-year-old man felt a painful 'pop' in the front of his elbow lifting a heavy box 8 days ago. He has antecubital bruising but, surprisingly, near-normal flexion and supination strength on exam. MRI shows the distal biceps tendon completely discontinuous at the radial tuberosity with an empty footprint, yet the stump is held at the level of the tuberosity with only minimal retraction; the lacertus fibrosus is intact and taut. How should this finding most appropriately direct management?

options.text: Because strength is preserved and the tendon has not retracted, call this a partial tear and manage nonoperatively with rehabilitation
Recognize a complete distal biceps tear tethered by an intact lacertus and refer for surgical repair, noting the intact lacertus masks the injury clinically and limits retraction
Attribute the symptoms to bicipitoradial bursitis as the primary diagnosis and treat with an image-guided corticosteroid injection
Defer any referral and plan for tendon grafting only if he presents again beyond a fixed 6-week window

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-biceps-lacert.explanation: The tendon is fully discontinuous with an empty footprint, so this is a COMPLETE tear regardless of preserved strength or minimal retraction. An intact lacertus fibrosus tethers the stump, limits proximal retraction, and can mask a complete tear both clinically (preserved flexion/supination) and on imaging — the classically missed complete tear — but it is still a surgical repair. Calling it a partial tear (A) is the trap: completeness is defined by discontinuity at the footprint, not by retraction or strength. Bicipitoradial bursitis is only a CLUE to scrutinize the insertion, not the diagnosis, and steroid injection (C) does not address a torn tendon. Surgical timing is a guideline, not a hard deadline: earlier anatomic repair is technically easier, and many primary repairs succeed at and beyond ~4-6 weeks, so withholding referral pending an arbitrary 6-week graft cutoff (D) is wrong — referral is appropriate now.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-biceps-lacert.stem: A 52-year-old roofer on chronic dialysis fell onto his hand and now cannot extend the elbow against gravity. MRI shows the triceps tendon torn across roughly 70% of its width at the olecranon insertion, with a fluid-filled gap involving the deep central fibers and a thin intact superficial expansion; a small avulsed osseous flake sits adjacent to the olecranon. How should this finding most appropriately direct management?

options.text: Because the superficial expansion is intact and it is not a full-thickness gap, label it triceps tendinosis and manage with activity modification alone
Diagnose olecranon bursitis as the cause of the weakness and arrange aspiration of the bursa
Withhold any surgical referral unless follow-up imaging beyond a fixed 6-week deadline shows further retraction

Recognize a high-grade partial triceps tear with extension weakness and refer for surgical repair, since deep-fiber tears with weakness are repaired and the intact superficial expansion makes the tear look milder than it is

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-biceps-lacert.explanation: The deep central triceps fibers fail first while the superficial expansion can stay intact, so a clinically near-complete injury can look only partial on imaging. A high-grade partial (commonly >50% width torn, or ANY tear with extension weakness against resistance) is a surgical lesion and should not be undercalled — here a ~70% deep-fiber tear with loss of active extension and an avulsed flake clinches it. Calling it tendinosis because the superficial layer is intact (A) is exactly the undercall to avoid, since the patient cannot extend against gravity. Olecranon bursitis (B) does not produce extension weakness or an insertional tendon gap and would not explain these findings. Surgical timing is a guideline, not a fixed deadline, and a repairable tendon tear should be referred now rather than waiting for an arbitrary 6-week cutoff before acting (C).

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-nerve-localiz.stem: A 22-year-old pitcher reports 5 weeks of medial elbow discomfort and a weak pinch grip; he cannot make a normal 'OK' sign and the distal thumb and index finger collapse. Sensation is fully intact throughout the hand. Elbow MRI shows STIR hyperintensity isolated to the pronator quadratus, flexor pollicis longus, and the index/middle portions of flexor digitorum profundus, with no muscle change in flexor carpi ulnaris, the ulnar intrinsics, or the extensor compartment, and no discrete compressive mass. Which interpretation is most appropriate?

options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve entrapment at the arcade of Frohse, supported by the supinator and extensor denervation pattern
Ulnar neuropathy at the cubital tunnel, because mild nerve T2 signal at the medial epicondyle is enough to confirm entrapment
An anterior interosseous nerve (Kiloh-Nevin) pattern; because an AIN palsy is frequently Parsonage-Turner rather than compression, pivot to EMG and observation before any decompression
An acute denervation pattern that must have arisen within the past few days given the muscle edema

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-nerve-localiz.explanation: The denervation pattern localizes the lesion: isolated edema in pronator quadratus, FPL, and the index/middle FDP with no sensory loss is the pure-motor anterior interosseous nerve (Kiloh-Nevin) distribution, and the inability to flex the distal thumb/index ('OK' sign) confirms it. The key teaching point is that an AIN palsy is frequently neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner) rather than mechanical compression, so the work-up pivots to EMG/observation and surgery is usually NOT indicated. Option A is wrong on localization and on a named misconception: PIN entrapment denervates the EXTENSOR compartment with radially-deviated wrist extension, not the PQ/FPL/FDP flexors, and the supinator is variably involved/often spared rather than a constant PIN feature. Option B applies the wrong nerve and the wrong rule: mild ulnar T2 brightness alone does not diagnose entrapment, which must be anchored to caliber change plus FCU/ulnar-FDP/intrinsic denervation against the contralateral side and the ipsilateral median internal control. Option D is wrong on the denervation timeline: there is NO MRI muscle change in the first roughly 2-4 weeks, then subacute T2/STIR edema appears, so the edema cannot date the injury to the past few days.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-nerve-localiz.stem: A 48-year-old recreational tennis player has 6 weeks of difficulty extending the fingers, and on exam the wrist extends with marked radial deviation while finger and thumb extension are weak; sensation over the dorsal hand is normal. Elbow MRI shows STIR hyperintensity throughout the extensor digitorum, extensor digiti minimi, extensor carpi ulnaris, and the thumb extensors, while the supinator shows no signal change; flexor carpi ulnaris and the ulnar intrinsics are normal. Which interpretation is most appropriate?

options.text: Posterior interosseous nerve entrapment at the arcade of Frohse; the spared supinator does not exclude it, because supinator denervation is variable and often absent rather than a constant feature
Ulnar neuropathy at the cubital tunnel, since the extensor-compartment edema reflects ulnar intrinsic denervation
An anterior interosseous nerve palsy, which always requires surgical decompression once muscle edema is seen
Denervation that must be only a few days old because muscle T2 hyperintensity is already present

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-nerve-localiz.explanation: The denervation pattern localizes the lesion: edema confined to the extensor compartment (extensor digitorum, EDM, ECU, thumb extensors) with motor finger drop and radially-deviated wrist extension (ECRL spared) is the posterior interosseous nerve, which is most commonly compressed at the arcade of Frohse. The trap is the normal supinator: its branches frequently arise proximal to the arcade, so the supinator is variably involved and often spared, and a normal supinator does NOT exclude PIN entrapment. Option B misapplies the ulnar nerve: ulnar neuropathy denervates FCU, the ulnar FDP, and the ulnar intrinsics, not the extensor compartment, and intrinsic edema would not explain an extensor-compartment pattern. Option C states a named misconception: an AIN palsy is frequently Parsonage-Turner rather than compression, so it usually does NOT need decompression and the work-up pivots to EMG/observation; this case is in any event a PIN, not AIN, distribution. Option D is wrong on the denervation timeline: there is NO MRI muscle change for roughly the first 2-4 weeks before subacute T2/STIR edema develops, so the edema cannot date the lesion to a few days.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-dontmiss-pre.stem: A 54-year-old plumber presents after a week of progressive posterior elbow swelling, low-grade fever, and a tender, warm, erythematous fluctuant olecranon prominence. An elbow MRI is obtained and shows a distended olecranon bursa with complex fluid, wall thickening, and surrounding soft-tissue edema; the underlying joint, marrow, and tendons are unremarkable. Which step is the most appropriate next management?

options.text: Report the complex bursal fluid as an aseptic (gout/repetitive-trauma) bursitis, since the joint and marrow are normal, and treat with compression and NSAIDs
Recommend MR arthrography to confirm whether the bursa communicates with the joint before any intervention
Interpret the complex bursal fluid and wall thickening as confidently excluding infection, and start oral anti-inflammatories alone
Aspirate the bursa for Gram stain, culture, and crystals, because MRI cannot reliably exclude septic bursitis in a warm, fluctuant bursa

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-dontmiss-pre.explanation: A red, warm, fluctuant olecranon bursa with systemic signs must be aspirated for Gram stain, culture, and crystals: MRI cannot reliably distinguish septic from aseptic bursitis, so imaging features alone cannot exclude infection (D). Calling it aseptic gout/trauma bursitis because the joint and marrow look normal (A) is unsafe, since septic bursitis can be superficial and spare the joint. MR arthrography (B) adds cost and delay, does not answer the infection question, and is not indicated here. Treating it as confidently non-infected on signal characteristics (C) is exactly the misconception to avoid: complex fluid and wall thickening occur in both septic and aseptic bursitis. The management-changing don't-miss step is the diagnostic aspiration.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-dontmiss-post.stem: A 39-year-old recreational weightlifter is sent in with 10 days of a tender, hot, red, fluctuant swelling over the point of the elbow and a temperature of 38.1 C. MRI demonstrates a fluid-filled, thick-walled olecranon bursa with heterogeneous internal signal and adjacent subcutaneous edema; the elbow joint, articular cartilage, and marrow signal are normal. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Conclude the heterogeneous bursal fluid reflects a chronic aseptic (repetitive-trauma) bursitis and manage with an elbow pad and activity modification
Aspirate the bursa and send fluid for Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis, since MRI cannot reliably exclude a septic bursitis
Treat empirically as a gout flare with colchicine alone on the basis of the imaging appearance, without sampling the fluid
Obtain a post-contrast MRI to determine from the enhancement pattern whether the bursa is infected before deciding on treatment

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-dontmiss-post.explanation: A hot, red, fluctuant olecranon bursa with fever requires aspiration for Gram stain, culture, and crystals because MRI cannot reliably separate septic from aseptic bursitis, and a normal-appearing joint and marrow do not exclude superficial infection (B). Labeling it a chronic aseptic repetitive-trauma bursitis from the imaging (A) or treating it empirically as gout without sampling (C) both commit the error that imaging or pattern recognition can exclude infection. A post-contrast MRI (D) cannot reliably make the septic-versus-aseptic distinction either, so it adds delay without answering the question. Fluid sampling is the definitive, management-changing step.

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-chronic-p.stem: A 24-year-old college pitcher has 4 months of medial elbow pain that builds across an outing without a single 'pop.' Coronal T2 FS MRI shows a thickened, heterogeneous UCL anterior bundle with a small ossific focus at the sublime tubercle and mild traction marrow change, but no fluid-bright full-thickness gap or retraction. On dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound the medial ulnohumeral joint opens 2 mm more than the contralateral asymptomatic elbow during applied valgus. He wants to keep pitching competitively. Which interpretation most appropriately drives the management conversation?

options.text: The thickening, sublime-tubercle ossification, and traction change indicate chronic valgus overload, and the side-to-side dynamic-US gapping difference adds the functional instability data that the static MRI cannot, so the repair-versus-reconstruction discussion is driven by chronicity, lesion features, and his demand together
The ossific focus at the sublime tubercle establishes an acute full-thickness UCL avulsion, so he should be referred directly for urgent reconstruction
Because the static MRI shows no full-thickness gap, the dynamic ultrasound is unnecessary and he can be cleared to keep pitching without further instability assessment
Any UCL that is thickened on MRI meets the threshold for reconstruction, so the imaging alone mandates Tommy John surgery regardless of the exam

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-chronic-p.explanation: The thickened, heterogeneous, ossified UCL with sublime-tubercle traction marrow change is the picture of CHRONIC valgus overload, not an acute event, and the painless gradual onset fits. Static MRI grades the ligament but cannot quantify functional instability; dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound supplies that, where a side-to-side medial-gapping difference on the order of ~1-2 mm (thresholds vary by study) is the commonly cited functional-instability range. Repair +/- internal brace versus reconstruction is then decided by location, surface, acute-vs-chronic tissue quality, and athlete demand together, not by any single MRI number (A). The ossific focus at the sublime tubercle is a chronic traction change, NOT proof of an acute full-thickness avulsion (B). The dynamic ultrasound is precisely what static MRI cannot replace for functional gapping, so skipping it and clearing him is wrong (C). Not every thickened UCL needs reconstruction; thickening alone is not a surgical threshold (D).

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-chronic-p.stem: A 27-year-old javelin thrower reports a season of progressively worsening medial elbow pain that fades with rest and returns with throwing, never with a discrete tearing event. Coronal T2 FS MRI shows an attenuated, irregularly thickened UCL anterior bundle with low-signal ossification along the sublime tubercle and adjacent traction marrow edema, without a fluid-filled full-thickness defect or fiber retraction. He asks whether the scan alone settles whether he needs surgery. Which interpretation most appropriately frames the next step?

options.text: The sublime-tubercle ossification proves an acute full-thickness UCL rupture, so he should bypass any further testing and proceed to reconstruction
Because any visible UCL thickening crosses the operative threshold, the MRI by itself dictates ligament reconstruction

The attenuation, thickening, ossification, and traction edema indicate chronic valgus overload, and adding dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound to measure side-to-side medial gapping supplies the functional-instability data the static MRI cannot, so management is decided by chronicity, lesion features, and demand together rather than a single MRI cutoff
Since the static MRI shows no full-thickness gap, dynamic ultrasound adds nothing and he can return to throwing without an instability assessment

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-ucl-chronic-p.explanation: Attenuation with irregular thickening, sublime-tubercle ossification, and traction marrow edema, plus a gradual, rest-responsive course, is the signature of CHRONIC valgus overload rather than an acute tear. The static MRI characterizes the ligament but cannot measure functional instability, so the appropriate next step is dynamic valgus-stress ultrasound; a side-to-side medial-gapping difference on the order of ~1-2 mm (thresholds vary by study) is the commonly cited functional-instability range. The decision between repair +/- internal brace and reconstruction then turns on location, surface, acute-vs-chronic tissue, and athlete demand together, not a single MRI cutoff (C). Sublime-tubercle ossification is a chronic traction change, not proof of an acute full-thickness rupture (A). Thickening alone is not an operative threshold, so the MRI cannot dictate reconstruction by itself (D refers to clearing him, B to mandating surgery)—and skipping the dynamic ultrasound discards exactly the functional gapping data static MRI cannot provide (D).

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-little-league.stem: A 12-year-old Little League pitcher has medial elbow pain that started after a hard throw two innings into a game; he could not finish pitching. Elbow MRI shows the medial epicondyle apophysis avulsed and displaced about 7 mm, with the bony fragment lying within the ulnohumeral joint and a moderate effusion. On the coronal images the trochlear ossification center is visible but the medial epicondyle is no longer in its normal position. What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Throwing rest alone, because a widened fluid-bright physis with marrow edema and no fragment heals nonoperatively
Refer for surgical fixation, because the fragment is displaced and incarcerated in the joint
Reassure that a visible trochlea without a visible medial epicondyle simply means the epicondyle has not yet ossified per the fixed CRITOE ages, and continue pitching
Treat as capitellar osteochondritis dissecans and obtain MR arthrography to grade the lesion

elbow-mri::prepost-elbow-q-little-league.explanation: This is a displaced, intra-articular (incarcerated) medial epicondyle avulsion, and either intra-articular incarceration OR valgus instability mandates surgical fixation regardless of the millimetre number; here the ~7 mm displacement also exceeds the widely taught ~5 mm throwing-athlete trigger, so the fragment must be retrieved and fixed rather than rested. The CRITOE clue confirms it: the trochlea never ossifies before the medial epicondyle, so 'seeing' the trochlea but not the epicondyle means the epicondyle has been avulsed — often into the joint.
